name: ABBOTSBURY
history: Named after Major Edward Abbott, granted an estate here in 1806; the land stayed rural until residential subdivision in the 1980s, and the suburb name was approved in 1984.

name: ABBOTSFORD
history: Named for the 1870s 'Abbotsford' estate on the Parramatta River, itself named after Sir Walter Scott's Scottish home. Once held Nestlé's chocolate works on the point.

name: ACACIA GARDENS
history: Named for the acacias (wattles), chosen to reflect the leafy character of this corner of Quakers Hill; a separate Blacktown suburb since 1996.

name: AGNES BANKS
history: Named by emancipist entrepreneur Andrew Thompson after his mother, Agnes, when he acquired the riverside farm in the early 1800s; the name spread from his property to the district.

name: AIRDS
history: Named by Governor Macquarie in 1810 for Airds in Appin, Argyll — the Scottish family estate of his wife Elizabeth Campbell; revived for a 1970s Housing Commission suburb.

name: ALEXANDRIA
history: Named in 1868, most likely for Princess Alexandra of Denmark. Sydney's workshop suburb — wool washes, brickyards and tanneries on the Waterloo swamps.

name: ALFORDS POINT
history: From 'Allfords Point' on 1890s parish maps, assumed to commemorate John and Jane Alford, landholders nearby from 1821; the suburb grew once the bridge crossed the Georges River in the 1970s.

name: ALLAMBIE HEIGHTS
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'a quiet resting place' — the hilltop suburb developed after the Second World War.

name: ALLAWAH
history: From an Aboriginal word recorded as meaning 'remain here' or 'sit down' — adopted when the Illawarra railway opened its station in 1925.

name: AMBARVALE
history: Named for 'Ambarvale', the 1816 farm of emancipated convict Samuel Larkin; reused for a 1970s–80s Campbelltown housing release.

name: ANGUS
history: Named for John Angus, Blacktown Shire president 1917–20; one of six North West Growth Area suburbs gazetted in 2020.

name: ANNANDALE
history: Named for the Annandale estate of First Fleet officer George Johnston — who led the Rum Rebellion arrest of Bligh — after his birthplace in Dumfriesshire, Scotland.

name: ANNANGROVE
history: Named for 'Annangrove House', recalling Annan in Scotland, birthplace of Lieutenant-Colonel George Johnston — his grandson settled here in 1893.

name: APPIN
history: Named by Macquarie in 1811 after Appin in Argyll, where his wife Elizabeth was born; settled from the same year.

name: ARCADIA
history: Named for the pastoral paradise of ancient Greece — a fitting boast for this nineteenth-century orcharding district.

name: ARNCLIFFE
history: Named in the 1840s after the Yorkshire village of Arncliffe; market gardens on the Cooks River flats turned railway suburb in 1884.

name: ARNDELL PARK
history: Named for Thomas Arndell, First Fleet assistant surgeon and early Hawkesbury magistrate; applied to a late-twentieth-century industrial-estate suburb.

name: ARTARMON
history: Named for the Artarmon estate of William Gore, Governor Bligh's provost-marshal, who stayed loyal to Bligh and was arrested and transported by the Rum Rebellion rebels in 1808; Gore named the estate for his family's Ardtarmon seat in County Sligo, Ireland.

name: ASHBURY
history: A 1920s coinage splicing its neighbours Ashfield and Canterbury — a garden suburb built largely between the wars.

name: ASHCROFT
history: Named for the Ashcroft family, graziers whose paddocks covered the site — proposed in 1964 to mark 150 years of the family's Liverpool grazing as Green Valley was built over their farms.

name: ASHFIELD
history: Named for Ashfield Park, the 1820s estate of Robert Campbell. Quong Tart's home turf; a railway suburb since 1855.

name: ASQUITH
history: Named about 1915 for H.H. Asquith, the British wartime prime minister.

name: AUBURN
history: Named from Oliver Goldsmith's poem The Deserted Village — 'Sweet Auburn, loveliest village of the plain'. Now one of Australia's most multicultural square miles.

name: AUDLEY
history: Named after Lord George Edward Audley, who camped here while surveying the Hacking River in 1863-64, years before the area became the pleasure-ground heart of the world's second national park, dedicated in 1879.

name: AUSTRAL
history: Named for the Austral Banking and Land Company, which subdivided the estate into small farms — austral is Latin for 'southern'; gazetted 1972.

name: AVALON BEACH
history: Named in 1921 by developer A.J. Small for the paradise isle of Arthurian legend — sold to buyers as exactly that.

name: BADGERYS CREEK
history: Named for James Badgery, who farmed his 'Exeter Farm' grant from 1806 — the creek took his name, then the district, now the Western Sydney airport's site.

name: BALGOWLAH
history: From a Gayamaygal word for 'north harbour', referring to its position relative to Port Jackson; the nearby Grotto Point rock engravings, depicting fish, sharks and a kangaroo, are thought to be around 1,000 years old.

name: BALGOWLAH HEIGHTS
history: The elevated southern half of Balgowlah ('north harbour'), subdivided between the wars.

name: BALMAIN
history: Named for surgeon William Balmain of the First Fleet, granted the peninsula in 1800. Shipyards and the deepest coal mine in Australia bred the Labor Party here in 1891.

name: BALMAIN EAST
history: The harbour tip of surgeon William Balmain's 1800 grant — the ferry steps here served the peninsula's shipyard workers for a century.

name: BANGOR
history: Named after Bangor in Wales by farmer Owen Jones in 1895 (his own village later became Menai); revived for the Crown subdivisions of the 1950s.

name: BANKSIA
history: Named for the banksia, itself named for Joseph Banks who botanised these Botany Bay shores in 1770; station opened 1906.

name: BANKSMEADOW
history: Named directly for Joseph Banks — the 'meadows' he described behind Botany Bay in 1770, later the colony's market-garden and now port-industrial flats.

name: BANKSTOWN
history: Named in 1795 by Governor Hunter for Sir Joseph Banks; Georges River farms became one of post-war Sydney's great migrant suburbs.

name: BANKSTOWN AERODROME
history: Gazetted around the airfield laid out in 1940 for wartime aircraft assembly — still one of Australia's busiest light-aviation fields.

name: BAR POINT
history: A descriptive name for the sandbar stretching to Bar Island — a Hawkesbury hazard recorded by 1833.

name: BARANGAROO
history: Named in 2006 for Barangaroo, the Cammeraygal woman and Bennelong's wife, who scorned the colony's pomp — chosen from a public competition to rename the old East Darling Harbour wharves at Millers Point.

name: BARDEN RIDGE
history: Named for Alfred Barden and his pioneering family of the Bangor district; split from Lucas Heights in 1996.

name: BARDIA
history: Named for Bardia Barracks at the old Ingleburn Army Camp, honouring the January 1941 Battle of Bardia — the first Australian battle of the Second World War; a suburb on the camp lands since 2009.

name: BARDWELL PARK
history: Named for Thomas Bardwell, whose 1830s farm straddled the valley creek that keeps his name; station opened with the East Hills line, 1931.

name: BARDWELL VALLEY
history: The creek-valley half of Bardwell's old farm, kept green by its golf course.

name: BARKER
history: Named for Thomas Barker (1799–1875), the industrialist who named the Maryland Estate in 1854; a 2020s Camden suburb.

name: BASS HILL
history: Named for surgeon-explorer George Bass, whose 1790s journeys with Flinders began on these Georges River reaches.

name: BAULKHAM HILLS
history: Recorded from about 1801-02, the name most likely comes from Scottish settler Andrew McDougall, who named the district after Buckholm Hills in Roxburghshire, Scotland; an alternative tradition says bullock teams would 'baulk' at the steep hill. Orange orchards covered the area for a century before the post-war subdivisions.

name: BAYVIEW
history: A literal 1880s subdivision name — the view over Pittwater that still sells it.

name: BEACON HILL
history: Named for the survey beacon on its summit, a trig point with one of the broadest views in the basin.

name: BEACONSFIELD
history: Named in 1881 for Benjamin Disraeli, Earl of Beaconsfield, who had died that year.

name: BEAUMONT HILLS
history: Thought to be named after the birthplace of the wife of developer A.V. Jennings; assigned in 2003.

name: BEECROFT
history: Named in the late 1880s after Hannah and Mary Beecroft, the sisters who were successive wives of Henry Copeland, NSW Minister for Lands; the orchard village's streets still recall them. Hannah, Mary and Copeland all became local street names.

name: BELFIELD
history: A 20th-century blend of its neighbours Belmore and Enfield, the name marking the locality midway between them. It entered postal use in the 1930s and was formally gazetted in 1977.

name: BELIMBLA PARK
history: Named in the 1940s when land between The Oaks and Oakdale was selected for a plantation of a eucalypt native to Belimbla Creek (in south-eastern NSW); now a rural-residential village of Wollondilly.

name: BELLA VISTA
history: Italian for 'beautiful view' — the Pearce family's 1890s homestead name for their hilltop farm, now the business-park heart of the Hills.

name: BELLEVUE HILL
history: French-flavoured 'beautiful view', formalised in the 1900s for the harbour-view heights of the old Cooper estate, Woollahra.

name: BELMORE
history: Named for the Earl of Belmore, governor of NSW, when the railway arrived in 1895. Home of the Bulldogs since 1908.

name: BELROSE
history: Named in the 1950s for the Christmas bush ('bell rose') that covered the forest ridges the new suburb cleared.

name: BENSVILLE
history: Named for 'Ben' — pioneer settler Benjamin Davis of the shipbuilding Davis family; a waterside settlement on Brisbane Water.

name: BERALA
history: From an Aboriginal word recorded as a name for the musk duck, applied when the station opened in 1912.

name: BERKSHIRE PARK
history: Believed named for the English county by grantee Richard Rouse, married at North Hinksey, then in Berkshire; small-farm country between Windsor and Penrith.

name: BEROWRA
history: From a Darug word usually given as 'place of many shells' or 'many winds' — bushland station country on the 1887 Northern Line.

name: BEROWRA HEIGHTS
history: The plateau half of Berowra, subdivided after the Second World War.

name: BEROWRA WATERS
history: The boatshed reach of Berowra Creek, where a punt has crossed since about 1903 and the present cable ferry since 1961.

name: BERRILEE
history: From a Dharug word of debated meaning, recorded from 1840 — linked variously to 'place of kangaroos' or to 'birra birra'; an orchard village of Hornsby Shire.

name: BEVERLEY PARK
history: Named through a farcical interwar council competition — two winners were dumped before resident Estelle Tucker's 'Beverley Park' stuck, for reasons never recorded; it wraps its golf course.

name: BEVERLY HILLS
history: Renamed in 1940 from Dumbleton — straight after Hollywood's Beverly Hills, to help sell the new brick bungalows.

name: BEXLEY
history: Named by landowner James Chandler in 1822 for Bexley in Kent, his home parish.

name: BEXLEY NORTH
history: The northern, East Hills-line half of Chandler's Bexley, gazetted with its 1931 station.

name: BICKLEY VALE
history: Named for 'Bickley Vale', a property name of Camden's Sidman family; assigned 1991.

name: BIDWILL
history: Named for John Carne Bidwill, colonial botanist who gave his name to the bunya pine; a 1970s Housing Commission suburb of Mount Druitt.

name: BILGOLA BEACH
history: From an Aboriginal word recorded as 'bilgola' — swirling waters; the palm-backed beach kept the name from an 1822 grant.

name: BILGOLA PLATEAU
history: The headland top above Bilgola's beach, subdivided in the 1950s–60s.

name: BILPIN
history: Originally 'Belpin', of uncertain origin — given as an Aboriginal word possibly meaning 'mountain', or linked to Archibald Bell Jr, who crossed the mountains here in 1823; the apple village of Bells Line of Road.

name: BIRCHGROVE
history: Named for the Birch Grove estate (1810s). Under its streets ran the Balmain colliery's 900-metre shafts.

name: BIRRONG
history: From an Aboriginal word for 'star' — adopted for the new suburb around 1927, with the railway station opening in 1928.

name: BLACKETT
history: Named for George Blackett, superintendent of the government cattle station at Rooty Hill in Macquarie's time; a 1970s Housing Commission suburb of Mount Druitt.

name: BLACKTOWN
history: From the 'Black Town' — the Native Institution settlement Macquarie established for Aboriginal families in 1823 on the Richmond road. The name, once casual, was kept by the railway and the city that grew.

name: BLACKWALL
history: Named in 1862 when shipbuilder Rock Davis called his new yard after Blackwall, the Thames shipbuilding reach.

name: BLAIR ATHOL
history: A Scottish estate name from Perthshire, carried by an 1880s Campbelltown property.

name: BLAIRMOUNT
history: A Scottish-flavoured estate name of the Campbelltown district; from 1928 Frank Young's Clydesdale stud — remembered in streets named for horse breeds.

name: BLAKEHURST
history: Named for the Blake family's 1840s land on Kogarah Bay.

name: BLAXLAND
history: Named for Gregory Blaxland of the 1813 Blue Mountains crossing; a mountains village on the railway from 1867.

name: BLAXLANDS RIDGE
history: Named for John de Marquett Blaxland, nephew of the explorer, who travelled this ridge to the Hunter in the 1820s.

name: BLIGH PARK
history: Named for Governor William Bligh, whose 1806 model farms lay along South Creek nearby; developed 1980s.

name: BLUE LABYRINTH
history: A bushwalkers' name of long standing for the maze of sandstone ridges south of the highway; adopted when the Blue Mountains National Park 'suburb' was subdivided in the 2020s.

name: BONDI
history: From the Dharug/Gadigal 'boondi' — water breaking over rocks. Subdivided from the 1850s O'Brien estate; the beach made it world-famous once daylight bathing became legal in 1903.

name: BONDI BEACH
history: Gazetted separately from Bondi proper; the beachfront strip grew with the trams from 1894 and the Pavilion of 1929.

name: BONDI JUNCTION
history: Named for the tram junction of 1881 where the Bondi and Waverley lines split. The trams are gone; 'shoot through like a Bondi tram' survives.

name: BONNET BAY
history: Named for the bonnet-shaped rock at the Woronora River bay; a 1960s–70s bushland subdivision.

name: BONNIE VALE
history: A 'bonnie' vale on Port Hacking, on the Simpson family's 1863 grant — the coiner unrecorded; a Depression shack settlement, now a national-park campground.

name: BONNYRIGG
history: Named after a Scottish place (commonly Bonnyrigg in Midlothian) via Bonnyrigg House, the c.1825 homestead of the Male Orphan School estate; the post-war migrant farms became 1970s suburbia.

name: BONNYRIGG HEIGHTS
history: From neighbouring Bonnyrigg — Midlothian's town via the Male Orphan School estate's Bonnyrigg House; the hillier 1980s subdivisions.

name: BOOKER BAY
history: Named for John Booker, early-1800s landowner on the bay between Woy Woy and Ettalong.

name: BOSSLEY PARK
history: Named for John Bossley, 1830s landholder of the Prospect Creek farms.

name: BOTANY
history: Named for Botany Bay, itself named by Cook in 1770 for Banks and Solander's plant hauls — the first name Europeans fixed on eastern Australia.

name: BOTANY BAY
history: The water itself — Cook's 1770 name for the bay of Banks and Solander's botanising, the first English name fixed on the continent's east.

name: BOUDDI
history: From the Darkinjung name of the headland, on maps from 1828 and commonly given as 'heart'; the national-park peninsula locality.

name: BOW BOWING
history: From Bow Bowing Creek — first recorded as 'Boro Borang', its meaning not reliably recorded; a late-1970s Campbelltown suburb.

name: BOWEN MOUNTAIN
history: Named for George Meares Countess Bowen, granted land on Burralow Creek below the mountain in 1834; a 1970s mountainside estate.

name: BOX HEAD
history: A mariners' name for the box-like headland guarding Broken Bay's western entrance.

name: BOX HILL
history: Named either for native box trees or for the hilltop 'hunting boxes' colonial gentlemen built for kangaroo-shooting — one survives on Bligh's old Copenhagen Farm; now a Hills housing-release heartland.

name: BRADBURY
history: Named for William Bradbury, whose 1820s 'Bradbury Park' inn served Campbelltown travellers.

name: BRADFIELD
history: Named in 2023 for J.J.C. Bradfield, engineer of the Harbour Bridge — the aerotropolis city rising beside the new western airport.

name: BREAKFAST POINT
history: Named after Captain John Hunter's party, who stopped to cook breakfast on this Parramatta River shore on 5 February 1788; the spot appears as 'Breakfast Point' on an 1799 survey and was gazetted a suburb in 1993.

name: BRERETON
history: One of the national-park localities gazetted in the 2020s, named by NPWS for a long-established feature within it — the origin of the Brereton name itself is not publicly recorded.

name: BRIGHTON-LE-SANDS
history: An 1880s resort branding: Brighton for England's seaside town, 'le-Sands' added to dodge postal confusion — Botany Bay's promenade suburb.

name: BRINGELLY
history: A name of disputed origin: applied by botanist George Caley in 1802, claimed both as an Aboriginal word and as a Welsh estate name connected to Judge-Advocate Ellis Bent; a farming parish from the 1810s.

name: BRONTE
history: Named for Lord Nelson, Duke of Bronté, via the 1830s Bronte House estate. The beach gully was a Victorian pleasure ground before the surf clubs claimed it.

name: BROOKLYN
history: Named by the 1880s railway contractors — partly for the American Brooklyn Bridge engineers then famous, on the Hawkesbury's river-town shore.

name: BROOKVALE
history: Named for the 1880s 'Brookvale' farm in the valley brook behind Manly; now the beaches' workshop and brewery quarter.

name: BROWNLOW HILL
history: Originally Lowes Hill, renamed by Macquarie in 1820 at Commissioner Bigge's request for Lord Brownlow; one of the great Cowpastures estates.

name: BUNDEENA
history: From a Dharawal word usually given as 'noise like thunder' — the surf on the Royal National Park's village beach.

name: BUNGARRIBEE
history: From the Darug name of Colonel John Campbell's 1822 horse-breeding estate — usually read as 'creek with cockatoos', though Campbell fancied it meant 'burial place of a king'; revived for a 2010s Blacktown suburb.

name: BURRAGORANG VALLEY
history: From a Gundungurra name tied in one account to 'the great kangaroo's place' and its creation story; the farming valley was drowned beneath Lake Burragorang when Warragamba Dam closed in 1960.

name: BURRANEER
history: From a Dharawal word for the point's locality on Port Hacking, kept by the 1910s subdivision.

name: BURWOOD
history: Named for Thomas Rowley's 1799 'Burwood Farm', after Burwood in Surrey; one of the original 1855 railway stations.

name: BURWOOD HEIGHTS
history: The southern rise of Rowley's Burwood farm, gazetted separately in the 1990s.

name: BUSBY
history: Named for James Busby, the pioneer viticulturist often called the father of Australian wine (and son of John Busby, builder of Busby's Bore); a 1960s-70s Green Valley Housing Commission estate suburb.

name: CABARITA
history: Long said to be an Aboriginal word for 'by the water', but that meaning is unsupported and rejected by Wangal custodians; the name more likely echoes Cabarita in Spain, from Surveyor-General Thomas Mitchell's Peninsular War service. The Parramatta River picnic point kept its 1880s pleasure grounds.

name: CABRAMATTA
history: From Cabrogal country — 'cobra' (a freshwater grub) + 'matta' (water place). Post-1975 Vietnamese settlement built Australia's largest South-East Asian community here.

name: CABRAMATTA WEST
history: The western half of Cabrogal country's 'cobra grub water place', gazetted as the suburb grew post-1975.

name: CADDENS
history: Named for the Cadden family, Penrith business pioneers; a separate suburb since 2012.

name: CAMBRIDGE GARDENS
history: Extends adjoining Cambridge Park's 1884 estate name; developed from the 1970s.

name: CAMBRIDGE PARK
history: On Phillip Parker King's 1831 grant, subdivided into farmlets in 1884 and named after the Duke of Cambridge, a friend of the King family.

name: CAMDEN
history: Named in 1805 for Lord Camden, the colonial secretary who granted John Macarthur the land 'Camden Park' for his merino experiment — the cradle of the wool industry.

name: CAMDEN PARK
history: The Macarthur estate itself: the 1835 Regency house where the family's merino dynasty lived remains in Macarthur hands.

name: CAMDEN SOUTH
history: Camden's southern growth, on the old dairy paddocks.

name: CAMELLIA
history: Named for the Camellia Grove nursery of Silas Sheather, whose 1850s riverbank camellias were famous across the colony.

name: CAMMERAY
history: Named for the Cammeraygal people of the lower north shore — one of the earliest Sydney suburbs to carry its people's name (1890s).

name: CAMPBELLTOWN
history: Founded by Macquarie in 1820 and named for his wife Elizabeth's family, the Campbells. Home of Australia's most famous ghost story, Fisher's Ghost (1826).

name: CAMPERDOWN
history: Named by Governor Bligh for the 1797 naval battle of Camperdown, part of his 1806 land grant. Its cemetery holds the Dunbar's dead and Eliza Donnithorne.

name: CAMPSIE
history: A Scottish name from the Campsie Fells, via the 1880s estate subdivision on the Canterbury sugar-plantation lands.

name: CANADA BAY
history: Named for the French-Canadian patriote exiles of the 1837–38 rebellions, transported and confined by this bay — Exile Bay beside it remembers them too.

name: CANLEY HEIGHTS
history: From Canley Vale's railway name, extended uphill by interwar subdividers.

name: CANLEY VALE
history: Named by Sir Henry Parkes after his birthplace, Canley near Coventry (Warwickshire), England; his estate here was 'Canley Grange', and the locality grew with the 1880s railway.

name: CANOELANDS
history: Named because Dharug people cut canoe bark from the tall stringybarks here — early timber-getters found the trees with neat panels removed; a remote orcharding ridge.

name: CANTERBURY
history: Named for the Canterbury Farm of 1793, after the English cathedral city; its sugar mill (1841) was the colony's first.

name: CARINGBAH
history: From an Aboriginal word for the pademelon wallaby, applied to the 1910s Sutherland railway estate.

name: CARINGBAH SOUTH
history: Caringbah's Port Hacking-side half, gazetted 2010s.

name: CARLINGFORD
history: Named in 1886 in honour of Lord Carlingford, British Under-Secretary of State for the Colonies (the name suggested after Mobb's Hill was likened to Carlingford in Ireland); orange orchards covered its Pennant Hills slopes for a century.

name: CARLTON
history: A St George railway suburb that developed after the line reached nearby Hurstville in 1884; the precise origin of the name 'Carlton' is not reliably documented.

name: CARMARTHEN
history: Revives the 'Carmarthen Hills' — Phillip's 1788 name for the northern Blue Mountains, honouring the Marquess of Carmarthen; a 2020s national-park locality.

name: CARNES HILL
history: Named for Lieutenant Thomas Carne, Liverpool's coroner to 1825, granted land nearby in 1819; a town-centre suburb of the south-west release area.

name: CARRAMAR
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'shade of trees', chosen for the 1920s station.

name: CARSS PARK
history: Named for William Carss, whose 1860s stone cottage and park on Kogarah Bay became the public reserve.

name: CARTWRIGHT
history: Named for Reverend Robert Cartwright, first rector of St Luke's Liverpool (1819–36); one of Green Valley's pioneer-named suburbs.

name: CASTLE COVE
history: Named for 'Innisfallen Castle', the 1900s harbourside mansion of Henry Willis that still crowns the cove.

name: CASTLE HILL
history: Named for the 1801 government farm's hill ('a castle on a hill' in early accounts). Site of the 1804 convict uprising that ended at Vinegar Hill.

name: CASTLECRAG
history: Named by Walter Burley and Marion Mahony Griffin for the crag of Edinburgh Castle, when they built their bush-suburb manifesto here from 1924.

name: CASTLEREAGH
history: One of Macquarie's five towns of 1810, named for Lord Castlereagh — Hawkesbury floodplain farming country.

name: CASULA
history: Named after 'Casula', the farm of settler Richard Guise, who was granted land here around 1809 and whose property was well established by Macquarie's 1810 tour.

name: CATHERINE FIELD
history: Named for Lieutenant-Governor George Molle's 1817 'Catherine Field' grant — which Catherine he honoured is not certainly recorded.

name: CATTAI
history: From an Aboriginal word of uncertain meaning, often given as 'swampy land'; an early Hawkesbury district where Assistant Surgeon Thomas Arndell's farm (variously spelled Caddie/Cattai) was established, now within Cattai National Park.

name: CAWDOR
history: Named by Macquarie, 'so much pleased with the beauty of the situation' he honoured 'my dearest Elizabeth's family' — the Campbells of Cawdor Castle; the Cowpastures' first village, predating Camden by two decades.

name: CECIL HILLS
history: Named for John Wylde's 1817 grant 'Cecil Hills', which the judge-advocate named after his family home Cecil Lodge at Cheshunt, Hertfordshire; the suburb (gazetted 1992) was farmland until the 1990s.

name: CECIL PARK
history: From 'Cecil Hills', Judge-Advocate John Wylde's 1817 farm named for his Hertfordshire family home; gazetted 1972, with Cecil Hills and Elizabeth Hills later split from it.

name: CENTENNIAL PARK
history: Created and named for the 1888 centenary of European settlement; the Commonwealth of Australia was proclaimed on its lawns on 1 January 1901.

name: CHATSWOOD
history: Named in the 1870s for Charlotte ('Chattie') Harnett, wife of the developer mayor — 'Chattie's Wood'.

name: CHATSWOOD WEST
history: Chattie's Wood west of the Pacific Highway, along the Lane Cove River bush.

name: CHEERO POINT
history: A 1918 holiday subdivision sold as the 'Cheero Point Estate' — the breezy name's origin unrecorded; absorbed into Mooney Mooney in 1977 and won back by local campaigning in 2003.

name: CHELTENHAM
history: Named in 1898 for the English spa town, on the Beecroft orchard ridge.

name: CHERRYBROOK
history: Named for the cherry orchards along its brook — Hills District farms until the 1980s.

name: CHESTER HILL
history: On land once part of John Thomas Campbell's 'Campbell Hill' estate, the name was coined by local resident Mrs H.A. McMillan as an inversion of her rejected suggestion 'Hillchester'; market gardens gave way to housing after the railway opened in 1924.

name: CHIFLEY
history: Named in 1964, when it was separated from Matraville, after Ben Chifley, the engine-driver Labor prime minister; part of the eastern suburbs' postwar public-housing estates.

name: CHIPPENDALE
history: Named for William Chippendale's 1819 land grant. Brewery and factory quarter whose workers' terraces now sit beside galleries.

name: CHIPPING NORTON
history: Named in the 1880s by William Alexander Long after the Oxfordshire market town where he had lived; sand and gravel pits worked from the 1950s were flooded to create the Chipping Norton Lakes after 1977.

name: CHISWICK
history: Named in the 1850s by Dr George Fortescue, who acquired an estate on the Parramatta River in 1851 and called it after the Thames-side London village of Chiswick.

name: CHULLORA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'flour' or 'bread' — railway workshop lands from 1924, wartime aircraft factories.

name: CHURCH POINT
history: Named for the 1872 Methodist chapel on the Pittwater point — the church went, the ferry wharf stayed.

name: CLAREMONT MEADOWS
history: From 'Claremont', the renamed property on the 1810 grant to Mary O'Connell — née Bligh, the governor's daughter; the estate opened in 1984.

name: CLARENDON
history: Named for the homestead of pioneer builder William Cox, after an English estate; the Hawkesbury village beside the Richmond line.

name: CLAREVILLE
history: An 1880s estate name on Pittwater's beach — likely for Clare in Ireland.

name: CLAYMORE
history: Named after the 'Claymore' property — the great Highland sword — adopted after Campbelltown rejected the local name Badgally for its unfortunate first syllable; a 1970s–80s public-housing suburb.

name: CLEMTON PARK
history: A mid-1920s subdivision named after Frederick Moore Clements, who made his fortune from 'Clements Tonic' and owned a large landholding here; the name Clemton Park was in use by 1925.

name: CLONTARF
history: Named after Dublin's Clontarf ('meadow of the bull'). Its picnic ground hosted the 1868 shooting of Prince Alfred — Australia's first royal assassination attempt.

name: CLOVELLY
history: Renamed 1913 for the Devon fishing village (formerly Little Coogee) — the long concrete-edged bay made it Sydney's safest swim.

name: CLYDE
history: Named for Scotland's shipbuilding river when the railway junction's works opened in the 1880s.

name: COASTERS RETREAT
history: Named for the 'coasters' — small trading vessels of the early 1800s that sheltered in this Pittwater bay to wait out weather; still boat-access-only.

name: COBBITTY
history: From a Dharawal word, kept by the 1820s church village on the Cowpastures.

name: COGRA BAY
history: The origin of the name is not definitively recorded — the word's form suggests a likely Aboriginal derivation (compare Kogarah, from a Sydney-language word for reeds), but no documented meaning survives. A boat-access-only community of waterfront homes on the Hawkesbury's north bank.

name: COLEBEE
history: Named for Colebee, the Boorooberongal (Dharug) man who with Nurragingy received the colony's first land grant to Aboriginal people — 30 acres on Richmond Road from Governor Macquarie in 1819, near the present suburb; rezoned 2005 around the Stonecutters Ridge estate.

name: COLLAROY
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'big reeds'; the suburb took the name of the steamer 'Collaroy' beached on its sand in 1881 — refloated, but the name stuck.

name: COLLAROY PLATEAU
history: The clifftop estate above the beached steamer's sands, interwar-subdivided.

name: COLYTON
history: Named after Colyton House in Devon, the family home of the wife of grantee William Cox Junior, whose 1819 land grant was later subdivided as the 'Village of Colyton' in 1842.

name: COMO
history: Named in the 1880s for Lake Como, Italy, by the railway's land promoters — the pleasure grounds and marina obliged.

name: CONCORD
history: Named in 1795, most likely for Concord, Massachusetts — a peace-making gesture between feuding officers and settlers.

name: CONCORD WEST
history: Concord's railway-side half, home of the 1880s state brickworks.

name: CONDELL PARK
history: Named after the 'Condell Park' estate of Ouseley Condell, an engineer who arrived in 1829 and received an adjoining land grant in 1830.

name: CONNELLS POINT
history: Named for the Connell family, 1810s Georges River grantees.

name: CONSTITUTION HILL
history: A hill on the Parramatta–Hawkesbury road bearing a name transferred from London's Constitution Hill; it was already known as Constitution Hill by 1804, when it was the rallying point for the Castle Hill convict rebellion.

name: COOGEE
history: From a Dharug word often given as 'koojah' — smelly place, for the seaweed piled on the beach. A Victorian resort with an English pleasure pier (1928–34) the ocean demolished.

name: CORNWALLIS
history: Named after the ship Marquis Cornwallis, which arrived in 1796 carrying early Hawkesbury settler Captain John Brabyn — among the earliest farming districts on the Hawkesbury lowlands near Windsor.

name: COTTAGE POINT
history: Named for the solitary 1880s fisherman's cottage where Cowan and Coal & Candle creeks meet.

name: COWAN
history: From a local Aboriginal word first recorded in the area in the 1820s, its meaning not definitely known (sometimes given as 'big water' or 'the other side'); Cowan grew as a railway village after the northern line opened, with the station operating from 1890.

name: CRANEBROOK
history: Named for the brook of the Cranebrook estate, 1800s Penrith grants.

name: CREMORNE
history: Named for the 1850s Cremorne Gardens pleasure grounds — themselves named after London's Cremorne Gardens in Chelsea.

name: CREMORNE POINT
history: The harbour point of the pleasure gardens, saved as reserve in 1905 by public campaign.

name: CROMER
history: Named for the Norfolk seaside town, on the old Dee Why lagoon farms.

name: CRONULLA
history: From the Dharawal 'kurranulla' — place of pink seashells. The only Sydney beach suburb on a railway line (1939).

name: CROWS NEST
history: Named for the 1820s 'Crow's Nest Cottage' of Edward Wollstonecraft, perched high like a ship's lookout.

name: CROYDON
history: Renamed Croydon in 1876 — after the London borough — when the existing Five Dock railway station was given the new name to end confusion, and the surrounding Croydon Park Estate was subdivided and auctioned the same year.

name: CROYDON PARK
history: Croydon's southern parkland quarter, between the wars.

name: CUMBERLAND REACH
history: Takes its name from the adjoining reach of the Hawkesbury River, via Phillip's 1788 County of Cumberland honouring the Duke of Cumberland; known in the early 1800s as Boston's Reach after settler John Boston.

name: CURL CURL
history: From an Aboriginal phrase recorded as 'curial curial' — possibly 'river of life' — for the lagoon behind the beach.

name: CURRANS HILL
history: Named for Michael Curran, a Narellan-district resident from the 1880s until his death in 1916 aged 90; farmland until subdivided for housing in the early 1990s.

name: CURRAWONG BEACH
history: Originally Little Mackerel Beach; renamed for the native currawong when the Labor Council of NSW opened its workers' holiday camp here in 1949 — Pittwater's union retreat.

name: DACEYVILLE
history: Named for politician John Rowland Dacey, whose dream it was: Australia's first public garden suburb, begun 1912.

name: DALEYS POINT
history: Presumably from an early landholder's surname, though the Daley is not well documented; the point is notable for a major Aboriginal rock-engraving site and the Beattie family shipyard of the early 1900s.

name: DANGAR ISLAND
history: Named for Henry Cary Dangar, who bought the island in 1864 — Phillip had called it Mullet Island in 1788 for the fish; subdivided for homes after Dangar's death, officially renamed 1922.

name: DARKES FOREST
history: Generally attributed to Surveyor Darke, who took charge of building the Illawarra Road through the district in the 1840s; an orcharding settlement from about 1890, much reduced by Woronora Dam catchment resumptions.

name: DARLING POINT
history: Named for Governor Darling's wife Eliza in the 1830s (originally 'Mrs Darling's Point'). Gothic villas of the colonial gentry crowned it.

name: DARLINGHURST
history: Named for Governor Darling (and possibly his wife Eliza) in the 1830s, when its ridge-top windmills gave way to grand terraces — and the gaol.

name: DARLINGTON
history: Named for Governor Darling via the 1830s Darlington estate — the tiny suburb the University of Sydney later swallowed.

name: DAVIDSON
history: Named for Sir Walter Davidson, NSW governor, when the forest district was reserved in the 1920s.

name: DAVISTOWN
history: Named for the Davis family — five sons of 1833 Irish immigrant William Davis became shipwrights whose Cockle Channel yards made this a major 19th-century Brisbane Water shipbuilding centre.

name: DAWES POINT
history: Named for Lieutenant William Dawes, who built his observatory here in 1788 and recorded the Sydney language with Patyegarang. The Gadigal name is Tar-ra.

name: DEAN PARK
history: Named for the family of William 'Lumpy' Dean (1776–1854), granted land beside Eastern Creek from 1817, whose family ran the Bush Inn on the Western Highway; developed as a planned suburb in the early 1980s.

name: DEE WHY
history: From a surveyor's 1815 field-book margin note 'DY' beside the lagoon — its meaning lost, every theory since unproven.

name: DENHAM COURT
history: Named for the Denham Court homestead on Judge-Advocate Richard Atkins' 1810 grant, after his family home at Denham, England; the estate became a noted 1820s–30s social centre and the suburb name was approved in 1976.

name: DENISTONE
history: Named for Denistone House, built in the 1830s by Dr Thomas Forster (who named it after his English birthplace) on land that had been Gregory Blaxland's Brush Farm estate.

name: DENISTONE EAST
history: Denistone's eastern orchard slopes, gazetted with the 1930s subdivisions.

name: DENISTONE WEST
history: The Ryde-side slice of the Blaxland estate.

name: DHARRUK
history: Named in the 1970s for the Dharug people whose country the Mount Druitt estates occupy — a rare official acknowledgment of its era.

name: DOLANS BAY
history: Named for the Dolan family — Patrick Dolan bought about 286 acres beside the bay in 1856 and his son Dominick added neighbouring land in the 1860s; the 'Dolans land' on Port Hacking's northern shore.

name: DOLLS POINT
history: A small point on the western shore of Botany Bay; the origin of the name 'Dolls Point' is uncertain, with local legend linking it to an escaped convict who sheltered in the area.

name: DOONSIDE
history: Named after the 'Doonside' property established by the Scottish-immigrant Crawford family in 1826, recalling Scotland's River Doon of Robert Burns's verse.

name: DOUBLE BAY
history: Named for its twin coves. Sydney's most fashionable shopping village since the 1950s European migration brought its cafés.

name: DOUGLAS PARK
history: Named after Dr Henry Grattan Douglass, a leading early landholder on the Nepean; the final 's' of his surname was lost, reputedly through a mapmaker's error.

name: DOVER HEIGHTS
history: Named for the white sea cliffs — Sydney's answer to Dover's — when the clifftop estates sold in the 1900s.

name: DRUMMOYNE
history: From a Gaelic name, 'flat-topped ridge', via the 1850s Drummoyne House estate. The Gladesville Bridge arch lands here.

name: DUFFYS FOREST
history: Named for Patrick Duffy, the 1850s timber-getter who worked these ridges.

name: DULWICH HILL
history: Named in the 1880s for London's Dulwich; earlier 'Wardell's Bush' after the murdered doctor whose estate it was.

name: DUNDAS
history: Named in 1799 for Henry Dundas, British war secretary — one of the colony's oldest district names.

name: DUNDAS VALLEY
history: The orchard valley of the Dundas district, Housing Commission-built in the 1950s.

name: DURAL
history: From a Dharug word 'dooral' recorded as 'smoking hollow tree' — timber and orchard country since the 1800s.

name: EAGLE VALE
history: Named from Eagle Creek and Valebrook on Thomas Clarkson's grant — his successor styled her address 'Eagle Farm, Eagle Vale'; the old name was revived in 1976 when the land was subdivided for housing.

name: EARLWOOD
history: Renamed in 1918 (from Forest Hill), combining the surname of the Earl brothers, local farmers on Wolli Creek, with that of former Canterbury mayor Wood.

name: EAST HILLS
history: The literal east hills of the Georges River bend — terminus of the 1931 railway.

name: EAST KILLARA
history: Killara's post-war eastern bushland extension.

name: EAST KURRAJONG
history: From the Dharug 'garajun', given as 'fishing line' — kurrajong bark made line and nets; the district east of the Hawkesbury grew from scattered farm settlements, its school taking the name in 1922.

name: EAST LINDFIELD
history: Lindfield's eastern half toward Middle Harbour's bush.

name: EAST RYDE
history: Ryde's eastern remnant, gazetted with the Lane Cove crossings.

name: EASTERN CREEK
history: Named for the creek marking the eastern edge of the Blacktown grants — now raceway and logistics country.

name: EASTGARDENS
history: A 1987 coinage for the shopping centre built on the British American Tobacco site — the newest kind of suburb name.

name: EASTLAKES
history: Named in the 1960s for the lakes reclaimed from the old Botany Swamps alongside it; the suburb rose on the former Rosebery Racecourse site, redeveloped after the track closed in 1961.

name: EASTWOOD
history: Named for the 'Eastwood' estate of the 1830s–40s. In Maria Ann Smith's orchard here, the Granny Smith apple seeded itself around 1868.

name: EBENEZER
history: Named by its 1809 Presbyterian settlers for the biblical 'stone of help' — their little church is Australia's oldest still in use.

name: EDENSOR PARK
history: Named for Edensor House, the estate of chemist John Brown Bossley, after the village of Edensor near Chatsworth, Derbyshire; developed for housing in the 1970s–80s.

name: EDGECLIFF
history: A descriptive name from its position on the cliff edge above Double Bay, taken from Edgecliff House (c.1861), home of Justice Edward Wise and later William Busby.

name: EDMONDSON PARK
history: Named for John Edmondson VC, the first Australian Victoria Cross of the Second World War, raised on these Liverpool farms.

name: ELANORA HEIGHTS
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'home by the sea', chosen by 1920s golf-estate developers.

name: ELDERSLIE
history: Named for explorer John Oxley's estate, granted in 1816 and called 'Ellerslie' (later Elderslie) after the Scottish village; the name carried over to the modern Camden suburb.

name: ELIZABETH BAY
history: Named for Elizabeth Macquarie, the governor's wife. Alexander Macleay's 54-acre garden estate of the 1830s became the apartment quarter of the 1920s.

name: ELIZABETH HILLS
history: Named for Lady Elizabeth Wylde, who managed the farm at neighbouring Cecil Hills from 1816 until her death in 1864; carved from Cecil Park farmland and gazetted in 2009.

name: ELLIS LANE
history: Named for the Ellis family — Solomon Ellis arrived in 1855 and his son Samuel farmed 'Fernleigh' in the old Cobbitty Paddocks; designated a Camden suburb in 1991, still rural-acreage country.

name: ELVINA BAY
history: Attributed to early-1900s Pittwater developer Herbert Fitzpatrick, said to have named the bay for an Elvina in his life — accounts differ between his daughter and the woman who introduced him to his wife; a water-access-only community on Pittwater's western shore.

name: EMERTON
history: Named for William Emert, German immigrant storekeeper and postmaster who bought Mount Druitt land in 1861; a Housing Commission suburb of the late 1960s–70s.

name: EMPIRE BAY
history: Originally 'Sorrento' after a 1905 estate; renamed Empire Bay in 1908 when the post office opened, because Victoria already had a Sorrento — 'Empire Bay' was unique in the Commonwealth.

name: EMU HEIGHTS
history: Above Emu Plains — where the 1790s explorers reported emus by the Nepean.

name: EMU PLAINS
history: Named in the 1790s for the emus on the river flats; Macquarie's government farm, then the railhead under the mountains.

name: ENFIELD
history: Named after Enfield in London; the name was in use from the mid-19th century, with the suburb developing on Thomas Hyndes' 'Adelaide Park' estate (purchased 1843) and gaining its own council in 1889.

name: ENGADINE
history: Named in 1890 by Charles McAlister for Switzerland's Engadine valley, which he thought the Woronora gorges resembled.

name: ENGLORIE PARK
history: Named for the 1880 Englorie Park House of greyhound breeder Alfred Park — originally 'Euglorie Park' after Euglo Station, corrupted by misspelling; a separate Campbelltown suburb since 2000.

name: ENMORE
history: Named for the 1830s 'Enmore House' of Captain Sylvester Browne — after Enmore in Somerset.

name: EPPING
history: Named in 1899 after Epping Forest in Essex, home district of early landowner William Midson. Orchard country on the Field of Mars common.

name: ERMINGTON
history: Named for the South Devon parish of Ermington by Major Edmund Lockyer, who from 1828 built a riverside estate of that name and subdivided the 'Village of Ermington' in 1841.

name: ERSKINE PARK
history: Named for the 1810s 'Erskine Park' estate of James Erskine, lieutenant-governor.

name: ERSKINEVILLE
history: Named for the 1830s 'Erskine Villa' of Wesleyan minister George Erskine; brick-pit and factory terraces turned inner-west village.

name: ESCHOL PARK
history: Named for the 1858 vineyard of Campbelltown postmaster William Fowler, who took the biblical 'Eshcol' — the valley of grapes; the Geographical Names Board approved the misspelt 'Eschol' in 1975, and its streets are named for grape varieties.

name: ETTALONG BEACH
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'drinking place' — the Brisbane Water resort beach.

name: EVELEIGH
history: Named for the Everleigh estate. Its railway workshops were the largest in the southern hemisphere and the spark of the 1917 Great Strike.

name: FAIRFIELD
history: Named after the estate of Captain John Horsley, who bought the land in 1840 and called it Fairfield after his family estate in Somerset, England; one of Australia's most multicultural cities since the 1970s.

name: FAIRFIELD EAST
history: Fairfield's eastern flats toward the Orphan School Creek.

name: FAIRFIELD HEIGHTS
history: The rise west of Kenyon's fair fields, interwar-subdivided.

name: FAIRFIELD WEST
history: Fairfield's postwar western estates.

name: FAIRLIGHT
history: Named for Henry Gilbert Smith's 1850s 'Fairlight House', after Fairlight in Sussex — the Manly founder's own home cove.

name: FAULCONBRIDGE
history: Named by Sir Henry Parkes for his mother's maiden name; his mountain home and grave are here.

name: FIDDLETOWN
history: Named as a local joke: in the 1890s the Henstock brothers and William Small took violin lessons and played the local dances, and an Arcadia neighbour laughingly dubbed their selection 'Fiddletown' — the name stuck, and residents defeated a 2003 attempt to abolish it.

name: FIVE DOCK
history: Named for the five natural rock 'docks' indenting the bay — noted by name from the 1790s.

name: FOREST GLEN
history: A descriptive name for its setting in the great Dural forests on Dharug country; pioneered by Francis Nicolson in the 1890s, still a small semi-rural pocket of Hornsby Shire.

name: FOREST LODGE
history: Named for the 1836 'Forest Lodge' villa among Glebe's trees.

name: FORESTVILLE
history: A literal name meaning 'town in the forest', on the edge of James French's old timber forest; the area's post office opened in 1920 as French's Forest Settlement and was renamed Forestville in 1947.

name: FREEMANS REACH
history: Origin uncertain: either the Hawkesbury reach settled by free men rather than convicts, or for settler brothers named Freeman — a millwright Samuel Freeman appears nearby in the 1828 census; the locality takes the river reach's name.

name: FRENCHS FOREST
history: Named for James French, who logged these ridges in the 1850s — the forest name outlived the trees and grew suburbs.

name: FRESHWATER
history: Named for the freshwater lagoon stream at the beach; in 1915 Duke Kahanamoku rode its surf and taught Australia to board-ride. Briefly renamed Harbord; the old name won back in 2008.

name: GABLES
history: Named for Robert Hurrell's dairy farm, so large it split into 'Blue Gables' and 'Red Gables'; gazetted in 2020 — the developers wanted 'The Gables', but naming rules disallow a leading 'The'.

name: GALSTON
history: Named for the Ayrshire town by Scottish orchardists of the 1880s gorge country.

name: GARIE
history: From a Dharawal word given as 'sleepy' — though one story credits a bushranger named Geaty who camped there; the Royal National Park surf beach with its Depression-era cabin communities.

name: GEORGES HALL
history: Named after 'Georges Hall', the farmhouse built by Colonel George Johnston on his 1798 land grant beside Prospect Creek near the Georges River.

name: GILEAD
history: A biblical name given by Sydney hatter Reuben Uther to his 1812 grant, recalling Gilead's wheat fields — his crop impressed Macquarie on an 1815 visit; the Mount Gilead homestead was restored by the Macarthur-Onslows.

name: GIRRAWEEN
history: From an Aboriginal word widely given as 'place of flowers' — applied to the 1880s railway workmen's estates.

name: GLADESVILLE
history: Named for John Glade's farm. Its 1964 bridge carried the longest concrete arch in the world when it opened.

name: GLEBE
history: Named for the church glebe (clergy lands) granted in 1790. Boom-era villas and terraces saved largely intact by 1970s green bans.

name: GLEDSWOOD HILLS
history: Named for the c.1830 Gledswood Homestead of the Chisholm family, on a grant to Gabriel Louis Marie Huon de Kerilleau, tutor to the Macarthur sons; gazetted as a Camden suburb in 2011.

name: GLEN ALPINE
history: Named for 'Glen Alpine', the 1820s homestead of Reverend Thomas Reddall, Campbelltown's first Anglican clergyman; developed around the golf course from the mid-1980s.

name: GLENBROOK
history: Named for Glenbrook Creek, the 'Glen Brook' named by Sir John Jamison in 1818; adopted as the railway station and village name in 1879, the eastern gateway to the lower Blue Mountains.

name: GLENDENNING
history: Named for William Glendenning, the local butcher whose slaughteryard worked Lamb Street in the early 1900s; declared a suburb in 1987 within the Mount Druitt development.

name: GLENFIELD
history: Named for Dr Charles Throsby's 1810s 'Glenfield' farm, after his Leicestershire home.

name: GLENHAVEN
history: A literal valley-haven name from the 1880s orchard settlement.

name: GLENMORE
history: Named for 'Glenmore', the 1825 residence of Henry Cox, son of the mountain road's builder, in the Mulgoa valley hills.

name: GLENMORE PARK
history: Also from Henry Cox's 1825 'Glenmore'; nearly named 'Peachtree' before Penrith Council chose the historical name — the estate opened in 1990 on old Mulgoa farmland.

name: GLENORIE
history: A Scottish-flavoured 1890s coinage for the orchard district's post office.

name: GLENWOOD
history: Named after the historic 'Glenwood Park' property (so called from the 1940s, formerly Norfolk Vale and then Sorrento); the residential estate opened in 1994 and the suburb was formally recognised in 1996.

name: GLOSSODIA
history: Named for the Glossodia waxlip orchids that grow naturally in the district; formerly Currency Creek, renamed in 1922 to avoid postal confusion with South Australia.

name: GOARRA
history: One of eleven localities carved from the former Royal National Park 'suburb' in 2022, named after Goarra Ridge within the park — apparently of Dharawal origin, though no recorded meaning could be verified.

name: GORDON
history: Named in 1879, most likely for Sir James Willoughby Gordon — the north shore line's orchard station turned garden suburb.

name: GRANTHAM FARM
history: Named after the Grantham Farm Estate, a subdivision of Lieutenant-Colonel Maurice O'Connell's 1810 'Riverstone' grant; gazetted as a separate suburb in 2020.

name: GRANVILLE
history: Named for Earl Granville, British Foreign Secretary, in 1880 — earlier 'Parramatta Junction', where NSW's first railway terminated in 1855. Remembered for the 1977 rail disaster.

name: GRASMERE
history: Named for Wordsworth's Lake District village via a Camden dairy estate.

name: GRAYS POINT
history: Named for surveyor John Gray, who held the Port Hacking point in the 1880s.

name: GREAT MACKEREL BEACH
history: The 'Mackerel' is fishermen's lore from at least 1922, with 'Great' distinguishing it from Little Mackerel (now Currawong); first a 1823 dairy farm of John Clark, later held by Martin Burke, 'the Father of Pittwater', subdivided from 1920.

name: GREEN POINT
history: A descriptive name for the green headland on Brisbane Water; the 'Green Point Estate' dates from an 1881 partition, orchard country before mid-century suburbanisation.

name: GREEN VALLEY
history: The literal green valley of the 1960s Housing Commission's great south-west estate.

name: GREENACRE
history: An estate-agent's pastoral promise of the 1910s on the Bankstown scrub.

name: GREENDALE
history: Named after the 1811 'Greendale' property — attributed either to Mary Birch's grant or George Wentworth's 'Greendale House'; a thriving wheat district to the 1860s, gazetted a suburb in 1972.

name: GREENFIELD PARK
history: Named after Greenfield Road, the main road through Fairfield's St Johns ward; declared a suburb in 1979.

name: GREENHILLS BEACH
history: From 'Green Hills', the historical name for the Cronulla dunes on Myles Dunphy's Kurnell map; gazetted in 2011 on former sand-mining land after a twenty-year battle over the site.

name: GREENWICH
history: Named for London's Greenwich on the Thames, like several Parramatta River localities, and reinforced by boatbuilder George Green, who built 'Greenwich House' on the Lane Cove River point in 1836.

name: GREGORY HILLS
history: Named for the hilly ground and the original St Gregory's Chapel — the suburb occupies the former St Gregory's College farm, given to the Marist Brothers in the 1920s; assigned 2008.

name: GREYSTANES
history: Named for the 'grey stanes' (Scots for stones) of Prospect Hill's basalt outcrops, after 'Greystanes House' built in 1836 by Nelson Simmons Lawson, son of explorer William Lawson.

name: GROSE VALE
history: Named after the Skuthorp family's 'Grose Vale' estate — ultimately for Lieutenant-Governor Francis Grose (1792–94), like the river and valley nearby.

name: GROSE WOLD
history: Commemorates Lieutenant-Governor Francis Grose paired with 'wold', Old English for open upland; a scattered rural settlement near the Grose River.

name: GUILDFORD
history: Named for the Surrey town via the 1830s 'Guildford' estate; a railway brickyards suburb from 1876.

name: GUILDFORD WEST
history: Guildford's western paddocks, postwar-built.

name: GUNDERMAN
history: From an Aboriginal word recorded as 'gundamain', given as 'hut on a stream' — kept on Major Mitchell's 1834 map under his instruction to retain Aboriginal names; lower Hawkesbury farm country.

name: GYMEA
history: From the Dharawal name of the gymea lily — the great crimson spear-flower of the Port Hacking ridges.

name: GYMEA BAY
history: The bay-end of the lily ridges, 1950s-built.

name: HABERFIELD
history: Named from the Ramsay family's 'Haberfield' estate (a family name); developed from 1901 as 'the Garden Suburb' — Australia's first planned one, every house different, every garden hedged.

name: HAMMONDVILLE
history: Named for Canon R.B.S. Hammond, whose depression-era settlement scheme built homes for evicted families here from 1932.

name: HARDYS BAY
history: Named for the Hardy family — locally said to be Harry Hardy, who kept a small vineyard and sold wine to residents; the Bouddi peninsula village grew around the bay's farms.

name: HARRINGTON PARK
history: Named for the ship Harrington, seized by convicts in Sydney Harbour in 1808 and later destroyed — Captain William Campbell named his 1813 compensation grant in the vessel's memory; developed from the 1990s.

name: HARRIS PARK
history: Named for surgeon John Harris, whose 1790s grants included Experiment Farm — the site of Australia's first land grant to ex-convict James Ruse.

name: HASSALL GROVE
history: Named for the pioneering Hassall family — most sources cite Rowland Hassall (1768–1820), Tahiti missionary turned Parramatta preacher, though some credit his son Thomas, the 'galloping parson'; a 1980s Housing Commission subdivision.

name: HAWKESBURY HEIGHTS
history: Named for its perch above the Hawkesbury River — the river itself named by Phillip in 1789 for Baron Hawkesbury; the Blue Mountains locality's name was formalised in 2004.

name: HAYMARKET
history: Named for the hay and corn markets moved here in the 1830s. Paddy's Markets, Chinatown's third home, and the Capitol Theatre share its blocks.

name: HEATHCOTE
history: Named by Surveyor-General Sir Thomas Mitchell in the 1830s after a fellow officer from his Peninsular War service; later the railway gateway to the Royal National Park.

name: HEBERSHAM
history: Honours Reginald Heber, Anglican Bishop of Calcutta, whose diocese included NSW — an 1829 church village of the name was planned but never built, and the Housing Commission revived it in the 1960s.

name: HECKENBERG
history: Named for the descendants of Captain Casper Heckenberg, settled 1840 and later famed as champion woodchoppers; one of Green Valley's six estate suburbs, gazetted 1972.

name: HELENSBURGH
history: The Camp Creek mining settlement renamed after coal was found in 1884 — by mine manager Charles Harper, for his daughter Helen or for the Scottish town, depending who you ask; grew around the Metropolitan Colliery.

name: HENLEY
history: Named for the Thames rowing town — the Parramatta River regatta reach beside the Gladesville Bridge.

name: HILLSDALE
history: A 1940s Housing Commission coinage — dales below the Bunnerong hills.

name: HINCHINBROOK
history: Named for Hinchinbrook Creek, which Judge Barron Field had named after Hinchingbrooke, the English estate of the Earls of Sandwich; subdivided from Green Valley and gazetted 1986.

name: HOBARTVILLE
history: Named after the Hobartville estate at Richmond (by 1816, presumably honouring Lord Hobart), whose surviving 1828 homestead was completed by William Cox junior; subdivided from the estate in 1966.

name: HOLROYD
history: Named for Arthur Holroyd, first mayor of the 1872 municipality — merged into Cumberland in 2016 but the suburb keeps his name.

name: HOLSWORTHY
history: Named for the Devon town via an 1810s grant; the army's vast training grounds since 1913, and the internment camp of the First World War.

name: HOMEBUSH
history: From the 1810s 'Home Bush' farm of D'Arcy Wentworth. Abattoir and brickpit country reborn as the Sydney 2000 Olympic site.

name: HOMEBUSH WEST
history: The flemington markets side of D'Arcy Wentworth's Home Bush.

name: HORNINGSEA PARK
history: Named for the 1819 estate of Lieutenant Joshua John Moore, after his birthplace Horningsea in Cambridgeshire — his 1830s house still stands; farmland until the 1996 subdivision.

name: HORNSBY
history: Named for constable Samuel Horne, rewarded with land here for capturing bushrangers in 1830 — the upper north shore's junction town.

name: HORNSBY HEIGHTS
history: Horne's reward lands uphill, postwar-suburbanised.

name: HORSFIELD BAY
history: Presumably commemorates a Horsfield family of Brisbane Water, though no authoritative account survives; a small waterside village on Woy Woy Inlet ringed by national park.

name: HORSLEY PARK
history: Named for the 19th-century 'Horsley' homestead built by Major George Weston on land descended from Col. George Johnston's grant, after the Weston family's West Horsley in Surrey.

name: HOXTON PARK
history: Named for London's Hoxton via the 1880s land boom estates.

name: HUNTERS HILL
history: Named for Governor John Hunter. Stone village built largely by Italian and French masons in the 1850s–70s; the 1971 Battlers for Kelly's Bush here sparked the world's first green ban.

name: HUNTINGWOOD
history: A composite of the tradition that the colony's first English-style hunt rode here and the Woods family's century-held 'Woods Estate'; gazetted 1979, largely industrial since.

name: HUNTLEYS COVE
history: Named for Alfred Huntley, who built Point House nearby in 1851; the waterside suburb (formerly 'Tarban') took the name Huntleys Cove after a residents' vote in 2002.

name: HUNTLEYS POINT
history: Named for Alfred Huntley, who built Point House here in 1851; the headland and its ferry wharf carry his name.

name: HURLSTONE PARK
history: Named (1910, replacing 'Fern Hill') after the nearby Hurlstone College, which founder John Kinloch named for his mother's maiden name, Hurlstone.

name: HURSTVILLE
history: From 'hurst' — wooded hill — coined for the 1880s railway town that became the St George district's capital and a centre of Chinese-Australian life.

name: HURSTVILLE GROVE
history: The grove-side slice of the wooded hill, by Oatley Bay.

name: ILLAWONG
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'between two waters' — the peninsula between Georges and Woronora rivers.

name: INGLEBURN
history: A Scottish-styled 1880s railway name — 'ingle burn', the hearth stream — on the Macquarie Fields grants.

name: INGLESIDE
history: Named for the 1880s 'Ingleside' house near Sydney's only gunpowder works — which exploded more than once before closing; the hearth-side name outlived the powder mills.

name: JAMISONTOWN
history: Named for Thomas Jamison, the First Fleet surgeon granted 1,000 acres on the Nepean here in 1805; his son Sir John Jamison later inherited the land and built nearby Regentville.

name: JANNALI
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'place of the moon', chosen for the 1931 railway station.

name: JIBBON
history: From the Dharawal 'djeebahn', given as 'sandbar at low tide' — the beach and headland at Bundeena's eastern end, rich in Dharawal heritage including the Jibbon Head rock engravings.

name: JORDAN SPRINGS
history: Named from the historic 'Jordan Springs' property on the Faithful family lands (recorded as Jordan Hill in 1869), with 'Springs' also nodding to the development's stormwater lakes; created on the former ADI munitions site and registered in 2011.

name: KANGAROO POINT
history: A literal 1810s name — kangaroos shot on the Georges River point.

name: KAREELA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'place of trees and water' (or south wind), applied to the 1960s Sutherland subdivision.

name: KARIONG
history: From a Darkinjung-country word given by the Geographical Names Board as 'meeting place' — earlier called Kendall Heights and Penang Mountain before Kariong was adopted around 1947.

name: KARIWOGA
history: An obscure name shared with Kariwoga Ridge near Kurrajong, on traditional Dharug land — Aboriginal in form, but no recorded origin or meaning could be verified.

name: KEARNS
history: Named for William Kearns, a 19th-century farmer whose property lay here; the suburb was created as part of Campbelltown's growth and named Kearns in the 1970s.

name: KELLYVILLE
history: Named for publican Hugh Kelly, whose 1800s inn anchored the crossroads — 'Kelly's Village' by the 1840s.

name: KELLYVILLE RIDGE
history: The ridge paddocks of Kelly's village, built out in the 2000s.

name: KEMPS CREEK
history: Named for Anthony Kemp, officer-trader of the Rum Corps era, whose grants lay along the creek.

name: KENSINGTON
history: Named in 1890 for the London borough — racecourse-edge estates on the Lachlan swamps, now UNSW's home.

name: KENTHURST
history: Renamed 1886 from 'Little Dural' — a wooded-hill name with Kent attached, for the orchard ridges.

name: KENTLYN
history: The Campbelltown district known as the Kent Farms was formally named 'Kent Lyn' in 1933, later compressed to Kentlyn — a name that grew from the farming community rather than a single namer.

name: KILLARA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'permanent' or 'always there' — an 1899 north shore line station between the orchards.

name: KILLARNEY HEIGHTS
history: Named after Killarney in Ireland via an early-20th-century 'Killarney' picnic ground on Middle Harbour; the suburb itself was subdivided in the 1960s, with Irish-themed street names.

name: KILLCARE
history: Probably a holiday-resort coinage meaning to 'kill care' — cast off one's worries — from the early-1900s subdivision of the Bouddi peninsula's fishing farms into holiday lots.

name: KILLCARE HEIGHTS
history: The ridge-top above Killcare, sharing the 'kill (one's) cares' holiday name.

name: KINCUMBER
history: From a Darkinjung-country word, 'kincumba', given as 'towards the rising sun' — Kincumba Mountain's summit, a traditional meeting place, catches the district's first morning light.

name: KINCUMBER SOUTH
history: Kincumber's rural southern neighbour, sharing the name given as 'towards the rising sun'.

name: KINGS LANGLEY
history: Named for the Hertfordshire village via Matthew Pearce's 1795 'Kings Langley' farm grant.

name: KINGS PARK
history: Named in 1987 for the residential estate created when the area was carved out of neighbouring Marayong on Blacktown's Richmond railway line.

name: KINGSFORD
history: Renamed 1936 for aviator Sir Charles Kingsford Smith — the tram terminus suburb beside his airport's flight path.

name: KINGSGROVE
history: From Hannah Laycock's 1804 'Kings Grove' farm, honouring Governor King who granted it.

name: KINGSWOOD
history: Named in 1887 for the family of Governor Philip Gidley King, who held land in the then-wooded area on the Penrith railway (previously known as Cross Roads).

name: KIRKHAM
history: Named for explorer John Oxley's estate — granted about 600 acres here in 1810, he called it 'Kirkham' after his Yorkshire birthplace; only his stables survive, but the suburb kept the name.

name: KIRRAWEE
history: From a Dharawal word, given by Sutherland Shire Council as 'place of white (sulphur-crested) cockatoos' (girrawee), though 'lengthy' is sometimes also cited; the name was adopted with the 1939 railway station on the ridge above the Princes Highway.

name: KIRRIBILLI
history: From 'kiarabilli' — 'good fishing spot'. Admiralty House and Kirribilli House made it the nation's most powerful cul-de-sac.

name: KOGARAH
history: From a Dharawal word for the place of rushes — 'kuggerah' — by the bay; the St George district's medical and railway heart.

name: KOGARAH BAY
history: The bay-shore half of the place of rushes.

name: KOOLEWONG
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'koala' — originally called Glenrock, the locality adopted the name the Railways chose for its station.

name: KU-RING-GAI CHASE
history: From 'Ku-ring-gai', a name coined by anthropologist John Fraser in 1892 for the region's Aboriginal people and now regarded as a fabricated tribal label; 'chase' means unfenced natural bushland. Gazetted as a reserve in 1894 after Eccleston Du Faur's long advocacy.

name: KURMOND
history: A portmanteau of its neighbours Kurrajong and Richmond, coined when the area — originally Longleat — was subdivided in the early twentieth century.

name: KURNELL
history: From a Dharawal name, recorded as 'Collonel' — the Endeavour's 1770 landing place and the Meeting Place of Gweagal and British stories.

name: KURRABA POINT
history: From an Aboriginal name for the Neutral Bay point, kept by its 1900s apartments.

name: KURRAJONG
history: From the Dharug name for the kurrajong trees that grew abundantly here, whose bark fibres made fishing nets, ropes and baskets; European-settled from the 1790s.

name: KURRAJONG HEIGHTS
history: The mountain-edge village above Kurrajong, earlier known as Northfield.

name: KURRAJONG HILLS
history: Previously Kurrajong North and Netley Hill — after residents rejected an official 'Netley Hill' in 1971, the community settled on Kurrajong Hills, gazetted 2004.

name: KYEEMAGH
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'beautiful dawn' — the market-garden corner where the Cooks River meets Botany Bay.

name: KYLE BAY
history: Named for the Kyle family, 1850s shipbuilders of the Georges River bay.

name: LA PEROUSE
history: Named for the French explorer Lapérouse, who arrived in Botany Bay six days after the First Fleet in 1788 and vanished forever after leaving. Home to one of Sydney's oldest continuous Aboriginal communities, on Guriwal country.

name: LAKEMBA
history: Named after the Fijian island of Lakeba, via an 1880s landowner's South Seas connections; since the 1970s the heart of Muslim Sydney.

name: LALOR PARK
history: Named for the local Lalor family — landowner George Pearson Lalor, a Blacktown shire councillor whose Seven Hills farmland was developed by the NSW Housing Commission from 1958-59 into one of the state's largest housing estates, its streets named after famous Australians.

name: LANE COVE
history: Named in the 1790s, probably for a colonial official or the lane-like reach of the river — the name predates clear records. Timber-getters' river, then the north shore's garden middle.

name: LANE COVE NORTH
history: Lane Cove's postwar northern half.

name: LANE COVE WEST
history: The river-flat industrial and garden corner of Lane Cove.

name: LANSDOWNE
history: Named for the Marquess of Lansdowne via the Lansdowne Bridge over Prospect Creek, built in 1834-35 and named by Governor Bourke.

name: LANSVALE
history: A 1950s suburb on the Georges River whose name blends those of neighbouring Lansdowne and Canley Vale, dating from when Bankstown's peripheral areas took on their own identities.

name: LAPSTONE
history: Named for the lap-sized rounded stones of the first mountain ascent — the railway's Lapstone Zig Zag of 1867 began here.

name: LAUGHTONDALE
history: Named for John Laughton, a sailor who jumped ship in Sydney and settled the lower Hawkesbury in the 1840s as boatbuilder and orchardist; the riverside village ran salt pans, mills and orchards.

name: LAVENDER BAY
history: Named for George Lavender, boatswain of the hulk Phoenix moored here in the 1820s; Brett Whiteley painted its curve for twenty years.

name: LEICHHARDT
history: Named for the explorer Ludwig Leichhardt, who vanished in 1848. Post-war Italian migration made Norton Street Australia's espresso capital.

name: LEN WATERS ESTATE
history: Named for Len Waters (1924–1993), Australia's first Aboriginal military pilot — 95 RAAF Kittyhawk sorties in the Second World War; created in 2009 on the old Hoxton Park Aerodrome.

name: LEONAY
history: Named for the riverside estate of winemaker Leo Buring and his wife Ida, 'Nay' — their names combined as 'Leonay' in 1920, golf course added 1938; gazetted for the suburb after a residents' poll around 1974.

name: LEPPINGTON
history: Named for the 1810s 'Leppington' estate of William Cordeaux, after a Yorkshire village.

name: LETHBRIDGE PARK
history: Named for Robert Copeland Lethbridge, settled in the district from 1806 and married into Governor King's family; laid out in the 1960s Mount Druitt public-housing scheme.

name: LEUMEAH
history: From a Dharawal word given as 'here I rest' — John Warby's 1810s farm name, kept by the railway.

name: LEWISHAM
history: Named for London's Lewisham via the 1830s estate of trader Joshua Frey Josephson.

name: LIBERTY GROVE
history: Named in echo of 'Liberty Plains', the district granted to the colony's first free settlers, who arrived on the Bellona in 1793; a master-planned 1990s development, a separate suburb since 1998.

name: LIDCOMBE
history: A 1914 blend of two mayors' names — Lidbury and Larcombe. Home of Rookwood, the largest operating Victorian-era cemetery on earth.

name: LILLI PILLI
history: Named for the lilly pilly trees of the Port Hacking point.

name: LILYFIELD
history: Likely named for the waterlilies of its creeks. Callan Park's Kirkbride asylum — light and gardens as treatment — opened on its shore in 1884.

name: LILYVALE
history: Began as an 1884 railway construction camp; its station opened in 1889 as 'Lilydale' and was renamed 'Lilyvale' within the year, though no explanation for the floral name survives. The area appears on an 1842 map as Bulgo.

name: LINDFIELD
history: Named for the Sussex village of Lindfield via an 1880s resident's home; a north shore line garden suburb.

name: LINLEY POINT
history: Named for the 1880s 'Linley' estate on the Lane Cove River point.

name: LITTLE BAY
history: The literal little bay south of Long Bay — site of the 1881 coast hospital where Sydney isolated its smallpox and plague patients.

name: LITTLE WOBBY
history: Said to take its name from the wobbegong — an Aboriginal word for the carpet sharks of the lower Hawkesbury; the foreshore community under the cliffs was also known as Tumbledown for its rock falls.

name: LIVERPOOL
history: Founded and named by Governor Macquarie in 1810 for the Earl of Liverpool — one of Australia's oldest towns, on Cabrogal country by the Georges River.

name: LLANDILO
history: Named after Llandeilo in Wales at the 1895 subdivision; the first grant here went to emancipist Samuel Terry in 1818, who called it Terry Brook.

name: LOFTUS
history: Named for Lord Augustus Loftus, NSW governor, when the Illawarra railway opened in 1886; the tramway museum keeps Sydney's lost trams here.

name: LONDONDERRY
history: Named for the Irish county/city, after the 'Londonderry' property granted to Thomas Kendell in 1831 in the rural Hawkesbury area south of Richmond.

name: LONG POINT
history: A descriptive name for the peninsula in a Georges River bend — informally Long Point or Long Nose Point through the nineteenth century, formalised in 1975.

name: LONGUEVILLE
history: Named for the 1840s 'Longueville' estate — a Norman-French flourish on the Lane Cove peninsula.

name: LOVETT BAY
history: Named for John Lovett, who settled this western Pittwater bay in 1836; the 1895 wharf and Flagstaff track made it an early picnic favourite.

name: LOWER PORTLAND
history: Downstream of Portland Head, honouring the Duke of Portland — in use from about 1805, with local lore adding a headland rock said to resemble the Duke; at the junction of the Colo and Hawkesbury.

name: LUCAS HEIGHTS
history: Named for 1820s miller John Lucas; since 1958 the home of Australia's only nuclear reactor.

name: LUDDENHAM
history: Named for a Kent village via the 1810s estates on the Northern Road.

name: LUGARNO
history: Named in 1843 by Surveyor-General Sir Thomas Mitchell for Lake Lugano, the Swiss-Italian lake the Georges River reach reminded him of, with a stray 'r' added to the spelling.

name: LURNEA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'resting place', applied to the 1960s Liverpool estates.

name: MACMASTERS BEACH
history: Named for Allan MacMaster, a Scottish immigrant of 1839 who farmed some 600 acres behind the Bouddi peninsula beach from 1855.

name: MACQUARIE FIELDS
history: Named for Governor Macquarie's 1810s field grants — the name long predates the Housing Commission estates that made it famous.

name: MACQUARIE LINKS
history: Named for Governor Macquarie with 'Links' for the championship golf course at its heart — a gated estate styled as an Australian Scottish links from 1996.

name: MACQUARIE PARK
history: Named for Macquarie University (1964), itself for the governor — the orchard belt that became Sydney's tech corridor.

name: MAIANBAR
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'deep tank' or 'waterhole', probably chosen by the Royal National Park trustees; adopted officially in 1951 over the old Fishermans Bay estate names.

name: MALABAR
history: Named for the SS Malabar, wrecked on Long Bay's rocks in fog in 1931 — the suburb adopted the ship that died on it.

name: MALDON
history: Grew around a Great Southern Railway station, originally Wilton, soon renamed Maldon — matching the Essex town, though no documented reason survives; its cement works opened in 1951.

name: MANLY
history: Named by Governor Phillip in 1788 for the 'manly confidence' of the Kay-ye-my people he met there. Gocher's illegal 1902 midday swim made its beach the birthplace of Australian surf culture.

name: MANLY VALE
history: The valley behind Phillip's 'manly' cove, market gardens until the postwar build-out.

name: MARAYLYA
history: Presumed Aboriginal, meaning unrecorded; originally 'Forrester' after a 1794 grantee, with Maraylya adopted by the Progress Association in the 1920s.

name: MARAYONG
history: From an Aboriginal word often linked to 'emu' country, with the 1922 station.

name: MARLEY
history: Named from Marley Beach and Marley Head south of Bundeena when the Royal National Park was split into localities in 2022 — the origin of 'Marley' itself is not reliably recorded.

name: MARLOW
history: A tiny boat-access settlement on the Hawkesbury's north bank, ferry-served for mail and groceries; the name's origin is not recorded in the GNB register — genuinely uncertain.

name: MAROOTA
history: From a Dharug word of the Hawkesbury sandstone country, recorded from the 1800s.

name: MAROUBRA
history: From the Dharawal 'merooberah' — place of thunder, for the surf. Sandhills hosted Australia's deadliest motor-racing circuit, the Olympia Speedway (1925–34).

name: MARRICKVILLE
history: Named for the 'Marrick' estate (after a Yorkshire village), subdivided 1855. Birthplace of Annette Kellerman; industrial valley turned migrant heartland on the Cooks River.

name: MARSDEN PARK
history: Named for Samuel Marsden, the 'flogging parson' and pastoralist whose 1810s flocks grazed these flats.

name: MARSFIELD
history: Named from Field of Mars — the 1792 soldier-settler common ('field of Mars' for the war god) that covered the district.

name: MASCOT
history: Renamed from North Botany in 1911: residents picked 'Ascot' after the local racecourse, but the postal authorities objected, so the council adapted it to 'Mascot'. Its 1920 aerodrome grew into Kingsford Smith Airport.

name: MASSIF RIDGE
history: One of the new Blue Mountains localities of 2024, named for Massif Ridge — a descriptively named ridge connecting The Massif with Woodford Main Ridge.

name: MATRAVILLE
history: Named for James Mario Matra, the First Fleet-era proposer of a New South Wales colony — soldier-settlement suburb of 1919.

name: MAYS HILL
history: Named for Thomas May, who bought land here in 1859 when the governor's domain at Parramatta was first subdivided.

name: MCCARRS CREEK
history: Named for the creek into Pittwater — though no settler McCarr is known: the name is believed a corruption of 'McGrath', a c.1902 photo being captioned 'McGrats Creek'. Suburb gazetted 2012.

name: MCGRATHS HILL
history: Named for James McGrath, an Irish convict transported in 1802 who bought land here around 1813 and helped build the Windsor roads.

name: MCMAHONS POINT
history: Named for Michael McMahon, an Irish-born brush and comb manufacturer who built his home on the headland in the 1860s and became a local alderman and mayor. Billy Blue's 1817 grant underlies it; Blues Point keeps his name.

name: MEADOWBANK
history: Named for 'Meadow Bank', the name surgeon William Balmain gave his 1794 river grant; the descriptive name was kept by the Bennett family's later estate and by the 1886 railway bridge and station.

name: MELONBA
history: From a Darug word given as the name of a wattle of the area — per the Geographical Names Board register; carved from Marsden Park and gazetted in 2020.

name: MELROSE PARK
history: Named after pioneer aviator Jimmy Melrose (1913-1936); the housing estate was established in 1937 and the suburb formally named Melrose Park in 1947.

name: MENAI
history: Named by Welsh settler Owen Jones for the Menai Strait — renamed from 'Bangor' when the post office clashed; its neighbour kept Bangor anyway.

name: MENANGLE
history: From a Dharawal word for the swamp lagoons — the Macarthurs' dairy village, with Australia's oldest country railway station (1863).

name: MENANGLE PARK
history: The racecourse flats of the Menangle lagoons.

name: MERRYLANDS
history: Named after the Merrylands estate of Arthur Todd Holroyd, who from 1855 called his local dairy 'Merrylands' after a family property in England.

name: MERRYLANDS WEST
history: Merrylands' postwar western paddocks.

name: MIDDLE COVE
history: The middle cove of Middle Harbour's three north-shore inlets, 1950s-built.

name: MIDDLE DURAL
history: The middle of the smoking-hollow-tree orchard country.

name: MIDDLE HARBOUR
history: The middle of Port Jackson's three arms, explored within weeks of 1788 — the GNB records the Aboriginal name 'Warring-Ga' (Warringa) as its previous name.

name: MIDDLETON GRANGE
