history: Named for Flight Sergeant Rawdon Middleton VC, who, mortally wounded over Turin in 1942, flew his crippled bomber back so his crew could bail out; gazetted 2005 with aviation street names.

name: MILLER
history: Named for Peter Miller, early Green Valley settler — one of the 1960s Housing Commission's suburbs.

name: MILLERS POINT
history: Named for the windmills of miller John Leighton ('Jack the Miller'), who from around 1814 ground the colony's grain on the ridge. A maritime workers' village for two centuries.

name: MILPERRA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'a gathering' — soldier-settler poultry farms of 1919 by the Georges River.

name: MILSONS PASSAGE
history: Named for Robert Milson — son of James of Milsons Point — who bought Milson Island and adjoining river land in the 1860s; the channel was named in 1976, the settlement gazetted 1995.

name: MILSONS POINT
history: Named for James Milson, the early fruit-grower who farmed the point. Luna Park's face has grinned from its shore since 1935.

name: MINCHINBURY
history: Named for Captain William Minchin's 1810s 'Minchinbury' grant — later the famous champagne vineyard, now the brewery's neighbour.

name: MINTO
history: Named after the Earl of Minto (Gilbert Elliot), Governor-General of India; the railway station here was renamed Minto in 1882, having first been Campbellfield after Macquarie's 1811 grant to William Redfern.

name: MINTO HEIGHTS
history: The hillier offshoot of Minto, gazetted 1976 to replace the old village of East Minto; like Minto it takes its name from the Earl of Minto (Gilbert Elliot), Governor-General of India.

name: MIRANDA
history: Named in the 1880s — most accounts say for Shakespeare's Miranda of The Tempest, chosen by the Holt-Sutherland estate's surveyors.

name: MONA VALE
history: Named for Robert Campbell's early-1800s 'Mona Vale' estate, said to honour a place in his native Scotland; today the crossroads town of the northern beaches.

name: MONTEREY
history: Named for California's Monterey by 1920s subdividers selling Botany Bay as the Pacific coast.

name: MOONEY MOONEY
history: From a Darkinjung-country name of uncertain meaning — linguists suggest 'kangaroo', fitting the kangaroo engravings of the nearby Aboriginal area; local lore offers 'many rivers'.

name: MOONEY MOONEY CREEK
history: The 34-kilometre watercourse locality gazetted in 1996 — the same Darkinjung name as its neighbour, most plausibly linked to 'kangaroo'.

name: MOORE PARK
history: Named for Charles Moore, the lord mayor who reserved it in 1866. The SCG, the showground and Sydney's sporting memory live on its sand-hill turf.

name: MOOREBANK
history: Named for Thomas Moore, boatbuilder and benefactor whose Georges River banks these were — Moore College inherits his name.

name: MORNING BAY
history: Charted as Morning Bay in Captain Sidney's 1868–72 survey; later known as Towlers Bay until the original name was restored in 1984. Suburb gazetted 2012.

name: MORTDALE
history: Named for Thomas Sutcliffe Mort — his estate dale on the Illawarra line, 1897.

name: MORTLAKE
history: Named for the Thames-side Mortlake of the boat race; gasworks village of the Australian Gas Light Company from 1884.

name: MOSMAN
history: Named for Archibald Mosman, whose 1830s whaling station worked the bay. The harbourside bushland became Taronga's home in 1916.

name: MOUNT ANNAN
history: Named by magistrate William Howe after his hometown of Annan in Dumfriesshire, Scotland; the hill is now home to the Australian Botanic Garden.

name: MOUNT COLAH
history: From an Aboriginal word — koala country on the climbing Northern Line.

name: MOUNT DRUITT
history: Named for Major George Druitt, Macquarie's chief engineer of roads, granted the mount's estate in 1821.

name: MOUNT HUNTER
history: Named for the hill Governor Hunter climbed during his 1795–96 visits to the Cowpastures wild cattle, marked on his own 1796 map.

name: MOUNT KURING-GAI
history: A railway township on the upper North Shore line, named (from 1892, becoming 'Mount Kuring-gai' in 1933) after the same 'Kuring-gai' label as the nearby national park — a term coined by anthropologist John Fraser in 1892 and now regarded as a fabricated tribal name rather than a genuine local people.

name: MOUNT LEWIS
history: Named for the local high point (the site of present-day Mount Lewis Reserve) on the ridge near Punchbowl; the origin of the name 'Lewis' itself is uncertain.

name: MOUNT PORTAL
history: Mount Portal is a clifftop lookout in Blue Mountains National Park near Glenbrook, overlooking Glenbrook Gorge and the Nepean River; the precise origin of the 'Portal' name is not well documented.

name: MOUNT PRITCHARD
history: Originally 'Mount Misery,' the area was subdivided by estate agent Hugh Pritchard as the Cabramatta Park Estate in 1913 and renamed Mount Pritchard in his honour in 1919.

name: MOUNT RIVERVIEW
history: A literal name — the mountain shelf viewing the Nepean.

name: MOUNT VERNON
history: Named after Mount Vernon, George Washington's Virginia estate, the name traces to the 1820 land grant in the area to Anthony Fenn Kemp.

name: MOUNT WHITE
history: Takes its name from the mount beside the old Peats Ferry Road, its summit marked by the White trig station — the origin of 'White' itself is not recorded.

name: MOUNTAIN LAGOON
history: A descriptive name for the rare perched lagoon on the ridge north of Bilpin — a mountain-top waterbody created by tilting along the Kurrajong Fault.

name: MOWBRAY PARK
history: Named for William Barker's 'Mowbray Park' estate near Picton (c.1884), after the Mowbray baronial family of Norman descent.

name: MULGOA
history: From a Dharug word for the black swan — the Cox family's 1810s valley estates under the mountains.

name: MULGRAVE
history: Named for the Earl of Mulgrave via Hawkesbury district grants.

name: NARELLAN
history: An 1810s name of debated origin — possibly Aboriginal, possibly an Irish estate — for the Cowpastures church town.

name: NARELLAN VALE
history: Narellan's 1990s vale estates.

name: NAREMBURN
history: Above Willoughby's brick-pit village once locally known as Central Township (or Dog Town); the name 'Naremburn', used for the district from the late 1800s, is of unverified origin, most often traced to the 'Merrenburn' estate of landowner Alexander Dodds, with rival 'near-a-burn' and Aboriginal 'forked-creek' theories.

name: NARRABEEN
history: From an Aboriginal word — recorded from 1801, its meaning debated ever since — for the great lagoon; immortalised by the Beach Boys' 'Surfin' USA'.

name: NARRAWEENA
history: From an Aboriginal phrase given as 'quiet place in the hills', chosen for the 1950s plateau estates.

name: NARWEE
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'sun', with the 1931 East Hills line station.

name: NELSON
history: Named for Admiral Horatio Nelson — reinforced by Governor Bligh, who served under Nelson at Copenhagen and named his neighbouring grant 'Copenhagen Farm'.

name: NEUTRAL BAY
history: Named by Governor Phillip, who designated the bay neutral anchorage for foreign ships — far enough from town to watch them. May Gibbs's Nutcote sits above it.

name: NEWINGTON
history: Named for the Blaxland family's 1810s 'Newington' estate (after their Kent home); armory depot, then the Sydney 2000 athletes' village.

name: NEWPORT
history: A literal 1880s name — the new port of Pittwater's steamers.

name: NEWTOWN
history: Named after the 'New Town' stores opened on the Sydney road in the 1830s. King Street follows an ancient track; the suburb keeps Eliza Donnithorne's Miss Havisham legend.

name: NIRIMBA FIELDS
history: From a Dharug word given as 'pelican' — via the former HMAS Nirimba naval air station (1953–1994) on the site; gazetted 2020.

name: NORMANHURST
history: Named about 1900 after civil engineer and technical-education advocate Norman Selfe, combined with 'hurst' (wooded hill); the railway station was renamed to end confusion with nearby Hornsby stations.

name: NORTH BALGOWLAH
history: North harbour's northern slopes, interwar-built.

name: NORTH BONDI
history: The headland end of the beach, home to the golf-course rock engravings — art older than the suburb's name by millennia.

name: NORTH CURL CURL
history: The lagoon-mouth end of 'curial curial'.

name: NORTH EPPING
history: Epping's bushland pocket across Devlins Creek.

name: NORTH HARBOUR
history: The smallest gazetted suburb on the harbour's northern arm — Phillip's 1788 refuge anchorage.

name: NORTH KELLYVILLE
history: The northern extension of Hugh Kelly's village.

name: NORTH MANLY
history: The brookside flats behind Manly's lagoon.

name: NORTH NARRABEEN
history: The lagoon-entrance surf corner of Narrabeen.

name: NORTH PARRAMATTA
history: The Female Factory and gaol precinct of Burramatta — colonial institutions clustered north of the river.

name: NORTH RICHMOND
history: Across the Hawkesbury from Macquarie-town Richmond, named via Phillip's 1789 Richmond Hill for the Duke of Richmond.

name: NORTH ROCKS
history: Named for the rocky 'north rocks' of Parramatta's sandstone ridges.

name: NORTH RYDE
history: Ryde's orchard north, now Macquarie Park's tech-corridor twin.

name: NORTH ST MARYS
history: The northern neighbour of St Marys and its 1840 church of St Mary Magdalene.

name: NORTH STRATHFIELD
history: The northern, Bakehouse-quarter slice of Strathfield's estates.

name: NORTH SYDNEY
history: The municipality of St Leonards renamed itself North Sydney in 1890. The Harbour Bridge's northern pylons landed here in 1932 and the office towers followed.

name: NORTH TURRAMURRA
history: The northern arm of the 'high hill' — Turramurra replaced 'Eastern Road' in 1890.

name: NORTH WAHROONGA
history: The northern part of 'our home', Wahroonga.

name: NORTH WILLOUGHBY
history: Willoughby's northern section — the parent name most likely Mitchell's tribute to Sir James Willoughby Gordon, under whom he served in the Peninsular War.

name: NORTHBRIDGE
history: Named for the spectacular 1889 'suspension bridge' (a steel arch in masonry dress) the land company built to sell its Middle Harbour estates.

name: NORTHMEAD
history: The north mead — meadow — of old Parramatta's government domain.

name: NORTHWOOD
history: The wooded Lane Cove point, 1880s estate of the Davy family.

name: NORWEST
history: A 1990s business-park coinage — the Hills' 'north-west' compressed into a brand.

name: OAKDALE
history: A literal oak-dale name from the Burragorang coal and farm valleys.

name: OAKHURST
history: Named after Landcom's 1980s 'The Oaks Estate' (in turn named for a historical residence on Hyatts Road); residents lobbied to adopt the name in place of the Board's original choice, 'Hassall'.

name: OAKVILLE
history: Oak-village, on the Hawkesbury district's turf farms.

name: OATLANDS
history: Named for the Surrey royal estate of Oatlands via the 1830s villa of the Bettington family.

name: OATLEY
history: Named for James Oatley, convict clockmaker who made Sydney's first town clock — granted these Georges River woods in 1833.

name: OLD GUILDFORD
history: The original village core of the Guildford estate.

name: OLD TOONGABBIE
history: The site of the 1792 government farm — the colony's third settlement, worked by convict gangs; from a Dharug word for 'place near water'.

name: ORAN PARK
history: Named for the 'Oran' estate, subdivided from Harrington Park around 1829; the origin of the name 'Oran' itself has never been firmly traced. Its motor-racing circuit (1962-2010) is now a town centre.

name: ORANGEVILLE
history: Named for the orange orchards of the Cowpastures hills.

name: ORCHARD HILLS
history: A literal name — the Nepean district's orchard slopes, half given to the defence depot.

name: OTFORD
history: Named after Otford in Kent ('Otta's ford') — settler John Hargrave reputedly named his farm for his old home; previously Bulgo, renamed in 1885.

name: OXFORD FALLS
history: Named after two waterfalls on Middle Creek in the Narrabeen lagoon catchment; gazetted as a suburb name in 1902.

name: OXLEY PARK
history: Named for John Oxley, surveyor-general and explorer, via the 1880s estates on the western road.

name: OYSTER BAY
history: A literal name — the Georges River bay's oyster leases fed Sydney from the 1870s.

name: PADDINGTON
history: Named after the London borough. The village grew in the 1840s to serve Victoria Barracks, then exploded into Sydney's finest terrace-house suburb in the 1870s–80s.

name: PADSTOW
history: Named for the Cornish fishing town via 1880s estate maps; built out when the East Hills line arrived in 1931.

name: PADSTOW HEIGHTS
history: Padstow's Georges River heights, postwar.

name: PAGEWOOD
history: Named in 1929 for Alderman Fred Page, Mayor of Botany, who died that year; later home to National Studios (1935) and a General Motors-Holden plant.

name: PALM BEACH
history: Named for its cabbage-tree palms. Subdivided 1912 as the summer republic of Sydney's establishment; the world knows its beach as Summer Bay.

name: PALONA
history: From a Dharawal word given as 'hawk' — via Palona Brook, one of the Lady Carrington Drive brooks given Dharawal bird names in the 1880s; assigned within Royal National Park in 2022.

name: PANANIA
history: A 1920s coinage of debated origin for the East Hills line estates — possibly from an Aboriginal word.

name: PARKLEA
history: Park-meadow estate branding on the Blacktown farms; the markets made it a byword.

name: PARRAMATTA
history: From the Burramattagal name Burramatta — 'place where the eels lie down'. Founded November 1788 as Rose Hill; the farms here saved the starving colony.

name: PATONGA
history: From a Darkinjung word generally given as meaning 'oyster'; a small fishing and holiday village near the entrance of Broken Bay.

name: PEAKHURST
history: Named after John Robert Peake, who bought farmland here from William Hebblewhite in 1838 and donated land for an early church; his name was adopted for the public school in 1871 and then the suburb.

name: PEAKHURST HEIGHTS
history: Peake's wood uphill, by the Georges River bends.

name: PEARL BEACH
history: Developed on the former Green Point holdings by C.R. and C.J. Staples (father and son), who named the subdivision 'Pearl Beach' around 1927-1928 and gave its streets gemstone names.

name: PEMULWUY
history: Named in 2004 for the Bidjigal resistance leader who raided these very Prospect frontier farms in the 1790s — the rare suburb named for the man who fought its founders.

name: PENDLE HILL
history: Named for Lancashire's Pendle Hill by mill-owning settlers; the 1924 station fixed it.

name: PENNANT HILLS
history: The origin is uncertain; the name 'Pennant (or Pendant) Hills' was in use from around 1800. It is popularly linked to signal pennants, though that station post-dates the name, and has also been attributed to the naturalist Thomas Pennant.

name: PENRITH
history: Named by the 1810s for the Cumberland (England) market town — the Nepean crossing town under the mountains.

name: PENSHURST
history: Named for Penshurst Place in Kent via the 1880s estates of the Illawarra line.

name: PETERSHAM
history: Named in 1793 for Petersham in Surrey — among Sydney's oldest suburb names; Portuguese Sydney's high street since the 1970s.

name: PHEGANS BAY
history: Named after early settler John L. Phegan — the bay was previously Burrawong Bay; a Woy Woy Inlet village.

name: PHILLIP BAY
history: Named for Governor Phillip — the little bay inside La Perouse.

name: PICKETTS VALLEY
history: Named after William Sampson Pickett, granted land here in 1830; a semi-rural valley near Avoca Beach.

name: PICNIC POINT
history: A literal name — the Georges River picnic grounds of the 1880s excursion steamers.

name: PICTON
history: Named for General Thomas Picton of Waterloo via the 1840s town on the Great South Road.

name: PITT TOWN
history: One of Macquarie's five Hawkesbury towns of 1810, named for William Pitt.

name: PITT TOWN BOTTOMS
history: The flood-plain 'bottoms' below Macquarie's Pitt Town.

name: PITTWATER
history: Named 'Pitt Water' by Governor Phillip in 1788 for William Pitt the Younger — said to be among the first places formally named in the colony.

name: PLEASURE POINT
history: Named for the scenic Georges River point — opened as the 'Pleasure Point Resort' in 1925, the Liverpool district's first waterside subdivision.

name: PLUMPTON
history: Originally Woodstock, where Walter Lamb ran a greyhound-coursing track; renamed in 1889 after Plumpton in England — itself a famous coursing venue.

name: POINT CLARE
history: Thought to be named after County Clare in Ireland, though not definitively documented; grew after the station opened in 1891.

name: POINT FREDERICK
history: Named for Frederick Hely, Superintendent of Convicts and early Brisbane Water landholder — the planned town of 'Point Frederick' was renamed Gosford in 1839, but the peninsula kept the name.

name: POINT PIPER
history: Named for Captain John Piper, the harbour's flamboyant naval officer whose 1820s villa 'Henrietta' entertained the colony into his bankruptcy.

name: PORT BOTANY
history: The container port built into Botany Bay from 1971 — the working answer to the harbour's retired wharves.

name: PORT HACKING
history: Named for Henry Hacking, First Fleet quartermaster and pilot — the southern ria he sounded in the 1790s. He also shot Pemulwuy.

name: POTTS HILL
history: Named for the Potts family farms around the 1880s water-supply reservoirs.

name: POTTS POINT
history: Named for Joseph Hyde Potts, a bank clerk who bought the point in 1830. Its 1830s villas made it Sydney's first 'suburb' in the modern sense.

name: PRAIRIEWOOD
history: From the Prairiewood Estate, a 1970s subdivision of Wetherill Park; Fairfield Council adopted the estate name in 1991.

name: PRESTONS
history: Named after a local Irish family, the Prestons, who ran the area's post office in the early 1900s; long known as 'Cross Roads', it was officially gazetted as Prestons in 1972.

name: PRETTY BEACH
history: An evidently descriptive name for the small beach near Brisbane Water's entrance; grew with the Bouddi holiday settlements of the early 1900s.

name: PROSPECT
history: Named by Phillip's 1788 exploring parties for the 'prospect' from its hill — the frontier of the 1790s farms and Pemulwuy's war.

name: PUNCHBOWL
history: Named for the punchbowl-shaped hollow of the 1790s 'Punch Bowl' farm on the Georges River road.

name: PUTNEY
history: Named for the Thames rowing village — the Parramatta River's regatta shore.

name: PYMBLE
history: Named for Robert Pymble, the 1820s orchardist whose ridge the railway climbed in 1890.

name: PYRMONT
history: Named after a German spa town by the Macarthur family. Quarries ('yellowblock' sandstone), the CSR sugar refinery and a century of wharves built — then rebuilt — the peninsula.

name: QUAKERS HILL
history: Believed to recall Quakers thought to have been among the area's early settlers, though the exact origin is undocumented; the railway station was renamed Quakers Hill in 1905 after Thomas Harvey's property was subdivided.

name: QUEENS PARK
history: Named for Queen Victoria when the park was carved from the Centennial lands in 1888.

name: QUEENSCLIFF
history: Named for the headland's royal-jubilee era estate (1880s) at Manly's north end.

name: RABY
history: Named for Alexander Riley's 'Raby' estate, granted in 1809 and named in honour of his mother, Margaret Raby; the Geographical Names Board approved the suburb name in 1976.

name: RAMSGATE
history: Named for the Kentish seaside town — Botany Bay's 1880s pleasure shore.

name: RAMSGATE BEACH
history: Ramsgate's beachfront strip, gazetted separately.

name: RANDWICK
history: Named by founding landowner Simeon Pearce for his Gloucestershire birthplace, Randwick. The racecourse (1833) made the suburb; the tram made the racecourse.

name: RAZORBACK
history: From the Razorback Range, its craggy spine likened to a razorback pig's bristled back; scene of the famous 1979 truckies' blockade.

name: REDFERN
history: Named for surgeon William Redfern, granted land here in 1817. Railway workshops drew workers, then Aboriginal families — by the 1970s it was the political heart of Black Australia.

name: REGENTS PARK
history: Named for London's Regent's Park via 1880s estate branding; the railway triangle made it industrial.

name: REGENTVILLE
history: Named for Sir John Jamison's grand mansion Regentville (1824–25, honouring the Prince Regent who had knighted him) above the Nepean.

name: REVESBY
history: Named for Revesby Abbey, Joseph Banks's Lincolnshire seat — Banks names blanket this district.

name: REVESBY HEIGHTS
history: Revesby's Georges River heights.

name: RHODES
history: Named for the 1820s 'Rhodes' farm of the Walker family, after their Yorkshire home; a century of industry, then apartment towers.

name: RICHARDS
history: Named for Benjamin Richards, founder of the 1878 Riverstone Meatworks; carved from Riverstone and gazetted in 2020.

name: RICHMOND
history: One of Macquarie's five towns (1810), named for the Duke of Richmond — Hawkesbury farm country with the RAAF base since 1925.

name: RICHMOND LOWLANDS
history: The flood lowlands of Macquarie's Richmond.

name: RIVERSTONE
history: Named for 'Riverston Farm', Lieutenant-Governor Maurice O'Connell's c.1810 grant (after his Irish birthplace); Benjamin Richards's 1878 meatworks later made it a company town.

name: RIVERVIEW
history: Named for the Lane Cove River view from the 1880s Jesuit college that dominates it.

name: RIVERWOOD
history: Renamed 1957 (from Herne Bay) for its Georges River woods — erasing the stigma of the wartime camp hospital.

name: ROCKDALE
history: Named 'Rockdale' on the suggestion of postmistress Mary Ann Geeves (adopted 1887), for the large rock hill nearby, replacing the earlier name Muddy Creek; municipality of the market gardens.

name: RODD POINT
history: Named for Brent Clements Rodd, the 1840s solicitor whose family held the Iron Cove point.

name: ROOKWOOD
history: Australia's great necropolis, opened 1867. The locality was renamed Rookwood in 1876 — after the title of W. H. Ainsworth's 1834 gothic novel — and now holds over a million graves.

name: ROOTY HILL
history: An 1800s name of debated origin — possibly the rooty ground of the governor's stock farm hill.

name: ROPES CROSSING
history: A crossing of Ropes Creek — the creek named for Anthony Rope, a First Fleet convict granted land on it; created in the mid-2000s on the old ADI munitions site.

name: ROSE BAY
history: Named for George Rose of the British Treasury. Flying boats to London lifted off its water from 1938 — Australia's first international airport.

name: ROSEBERY
history: Named for Lord Rosebery, British PM, via the 1910s model-suburb estates by the racecourse.

name: ROSEHILL
history: Keeps Governor Phillip's original 1788 name for Parramatta, 'Rose Hill', honouring a Treasury official. Elizabeth Farm, Australia's oldest house, stands here.

name: ROSELANDS
history: A 1960s coinage for the shopping-centre suburb built on Roselands golf course.

name: ROSEMEADOW
history: Named after Thomas Rose, conditionally pardoned convict, baker and Appin-district pioneer — not after a meadow of roses; officially named 1976.

name: ROSEVILLE
history: Named for the 1880s 'Rose Villa' on the north shore ridge.

name: ROSEVILLE CHASE
history: Roseville's Middle Harbour 'chase' — hunting-ground styled — slopes.

name: ROSSMORE
history: Named after the local Rossmoor horse stud, believed named for Rossmore Lodge, the famous County Kildare stud; gazetted 1972.

name: ROUSE HILL
history: Named for Richard Rouse, whose 1813 house still stands. The battle of Vinegar Hill (1804) was fought nearby — Australia's only convict rebellion.

name: ROZELLE
history: Named for the rosellas shot for sport on the bay. Its tram depot, power station and goods yards made it Balmain's working twin.

name: RUSE
history: Named for James Ruse, the convict farmer of Experiment Farm — a 1960s Campbelltown estate on his later farming country.

name: RUSHCUTTERS BAY
history: A literal name from 1788, when convicts cut rushes here for the colony's first thatched roofs. The killing of two rush-cutters by Aboriginal people in May 1788 was long linked to this bay, but historians now place that incident elsewhere up the harbour (likely toward Darling Harbour).

name: RUSSELL LEA
history: Named after 'Russell Lea', the Iron Cove estate and Italianate mansion of pastoralist and politician Russell Barton (1830-1916); 'lea' means meadow.

name: RYDALMERE
history: Named after Rydal in England's Lake District by subdividers in the 1880s; earlier 'The Vineyard'. The Female Orphan School (1818) is Australia's oldest three-storey building.

name: RYDE
history: Named after the town on the Isle of Wight. With Parramatta, the colony's first orchard country — the Granny Smith's wider home.

name: SACKVILLE
history: From Sackville Reach of the Hawkesbury, honouring Viscount Sackville, Secretary of State for the Colonies; farmed from 1803.

name: SACKVILLE NORTH
history: The farming settlement on the opposite bank of Sackville Reach.

name: SADLEIR
history: Named after Richard Sadleir, naval officer and first Mayor of Liverpool (1872); a Green Valley Housing Commission estate suburb gazetted in 1972.

name: SANDRINGHAM
history: Named for the royal Norfolk estate — Botany Bay's 1880s bathing shore.

name: SANDY POINT
history: A literal name for the sandy Georges River point opposite Picnic Point — a small riverside enclave reached by one road through the bush, subdivided in the 1950s.

name: SANS SOUCI
history: French 'without care' — named for the 1850s estate of politician Thomas Holt, borrowing Frederick the Great's palace name for Botany Bay's pleasure shore.

name: SARATOGA
history: Suggested in 1910 by local resident Mrs Hawkesworth, who had been impressed by Saratoga Springs, New York; citrus country settled from 1863.

name: SCHEYVILLE
history: Named in 1907 for William Francis Schey, who established the government agricultural training farm here; the name endures in the national park.

name: SCHOFIELDS
history: Named after John Schofield, an emancipist silk weaver who farmed here from the 1820s-40s; a platform known as Schofield's Siding was built on his land after the railway opened, and the name stuck.

name: SCOTLAND ISLAND
history: Named by emancipist Andrew Thompson for his homeland when he ran salt pans here in the 1800s — Pittwater's boat-access island village.

name: SEAFORTH
history: Named after Loch Seaforth in Scotland by the 1906 subdividers, above the Spit where the punt — and the never-built railway — crossed.

name: SEFTON
history: Named after Sefton Park, James Wood's 1839 land grant, which he named after the village of Sefton in Lancashire, England.

name: SEVEN HILLS
history: Named for the seven hills counted on the 1790s road west — Matthew Pearce's grant district.

name: SHALVEY
history: Named for Patrick Shalvey, the butcher who grazed these paddocks from 1906 to supply his city shops; a Mount Druitt suburb from about 1971.

name: SHANES PARK
history: From 'Shane's Park', surgeon John Harris's South Creek estate (1805) — the 'Shane' thought to be the Ulster Irish form of John, or Shane's Castle near his native Magherafelt; the true reason is unrecorded.

name: SILVERDALE
history: A silver-dale name for the Burragorang road farms.

name: SILVERWATER
history: Of uncertain origin, the name was used when this part of the Newington Estate was subdivided in 1883; it may refer to silvery reflections off the nearby Parramatta River.

name: SINGLETONS MILL
history: Named for the tidal flour mill built on the Hawkesbury in the 1820s by James and Benjamin Singleton — Benjamin also named the Hunter Valley's Singleton; the mill stood until about 1900.

name: SMEATON GRANGE
history: Named for the estate on the 1816 grant to explorers William Hovell and Charles Throsby; rural until rezoned for industry in the 1990s.

name: SMITHFIELD
history: Named for London's meat market — the 1830s village drove cattle to Sydney along the Horsley road.

name: SOUTH COOGEE
history: The clifftop south of the smelly-seaweed bay.

name: SOUTH GRANVILLE
history: Granville's southern works district.

name: SOUTH HURSTVILLE
history: The wooded hill's southern village.

name: SOUTH MAROOTA
history: From a Darug word given as 'pathway' or 'place of much water', referring to the springs; Maroota was in use by 1827.

name: SOUTH PENRITH
history: Penrith's postwar southern estates.

name: SOUTH TURRAMURRA
history: The high-ridge bush edge of Turramurra.

name: SOUTH WENTWORTHVILLE
history: Wentworthville's southern half.

name: SOUTH WINDSOR
history: Macquarie-town Windsor's southern flats.

name: SPENCER
history: Believed named for Earl Spencer, First Lord of the Admiralty — though not conclusively documented; the village at the Hawkesbury–Mangrove Creek junction was earlier 'Fernleigh'.

name: SPRING FARM
history: An echo of the farming history beside the Nepean floodplain, recalled in Springs Road's orchards and vineyards; proclaimed 1992, built out from 2007.

name: SPRINGWOOD
history: Named by Governor Macquarie himself in 1815 for the 'spring' and 'wood' of his mountain camp — the mid-mountains' town.

name: ST ANDREWS
history: Named after the farm of Scottish emancipist Andrew Thompson, who called his property after St Andrew, patron saint of Scotland; the Campbelltown suburb was gazetted in 1976 and its streets carry Scottish place-names.

name: ST CLAIR
history: A western-Sydney release suburb whose name has no historic link to the locality — 'St Clair' was the name of a 1970 housing development by the Latex Finance Company, and the Geographical Names Board confirmed it as a suburb name in 1981.

name: ST HELENS PARK
history: Named after 'St Helens', an 1887 Gothic mansion built for Sydney solicitor George Charles Westgarth that still stands on St Helens Park Drive; the surrounding suburb was named in 1976.

name: ST HUBERTS ISLAND
history: Named for St Hubert, patron saint of hunters; originally Bird Island, it was acquired in 1855 by Father Cornelius Coghlan, an Irish Dominican priest serving the Brisbane Water district, and was transformed into a canal estate in the 1970s.

name: ST IVES
history: Formerly Rosedale, renamed St Ives around 1900 — possibly after the Cornish town of St Ives, or after local politician Isaac Ellis Ives, who had championed a post office and school for the district.

name: ST IVES CHASE
history: St Ives' bushland 'chase' toward Ku-ring-gai Chase.

name: ST JOHNS PARK
history: Named after the old Parish of St John, in which it sits in the Cabramatta district, and a 19th-century St Johns Farm; the suburb's St John's Anglican church came later, in 1915.

name: ST LEONARDS
history: Named in the 1830s — either after Lord Sydney of St Leonards or, per Surveyor-General Thomas Mitchell, after the English town; the name once covered the whole north-shore municipality, and the hospital and towers keep it alive today.

name: ST MARYS
history: Named for its 1840 St Mary Magdalene church; the munitions factories of two world wars built the suburb around it.

name: ST PETERS
history: Named for its 1839 St Peters church on the Cooks River road; the brickpits beside it fired half of Victorian Sydney, and their chimneys still stand in Sydney Park.

name: STANHOPE GARDENS
history: From 'Stanhope Farm', John Hillas's 1804 grant and inn on Windsor Road; residents petitioned to keep the Stanhope name when the suburb was gazetted in 1996.

name: STANMORE
history: Named for the 1830s 'Stanmore' estate of merchant John Jones, after the Middlesex village.

name: STRATHFIELD
history: Named after the 1860s mansion 'Strathfield' (built 1868 as 'Strathfieldsaye'), ultimately echoing the Duke of Wellington's English estate Strathfield Saye; the railway junction made it the area's mansion belt.

name: STRATHFIELD SOUTH
history: Strathfield's plainer southern half, along the Cooks River.

name: SUMMER HILL
history: An inner-west village whose name appears from the 1870s (the station opened 1879), variously linked to a local 'Summer Hill' property or to NSW Corps officer Nicholas Bayly's nearby 'Sunning Hill' grant.

name: SUN VALLEY
history: Named in the 1960s for its subdivision — the lower mountains valley where Blaxland, Wentworth and Lawson camped in 1813, earlier Fitzgerald's Valley.

name: SURRY HILLS
history: Named after Surrey in England via the 1790s 'Surry Hills' estate. Sandhills became slums, slums became Ruth Park's pages, and the terraces survived to gentrify.

name: SUTHERLAND
history: Named for Forby Sutherland, the Endeavour seaman who died at Botany Bay in 1770 — the first Briton buried in eastern Australia — via the shire and its 1885 railway town.

name: SYDENHAM
history: Named for London's Sydenham via 1840s estates; the airport's loudest neighbour.

name: SYDNEY
history: Named by Governor Phillip in 1788 for the British Home Secretary, Lord Sydney. The city grid still follows the bullock tracks and stream lines of the first settlement at Warrane/Sydney Cove.

name: SYDNEY OLYMPIC PARK
history: Gazetted 2009 from Homebush Bay — the remediated heart of the 2000 Games, where 112,000 watched Cathy Freeman run.

name: SYLVANIA
history: Latin 'forest land' — 1880s estate Latin for the wooded Georges River shore.

name: SYLVANIA WATERS
history: The 1960s canal-estate addition — Australia's television byword for waterfront suburbia after the 1992 documentary.

name: TALLAWONG
history: From a Dharug word given as 'apple gum tree' — gazetted 2020 and named after the Sydney Metro terminus opened there in 2019.

name: TAMARAMA
history: From 'Gamma Gamma', a recorded Aboriginal name for the cove. Briefly home to Wonderland City (1906–11), the southern hemisphere's biggest amusement park.

name: TAREN POINT
history: A Georges River point earlier known as Commons (or Cummins) Point after a local resident; the origin of the name 'Taren Point' is unrecorded. It kept a quiet character through its sawmill and oyster decades.

name: TASCOTT
history: A contraction of 'T.A.Scott' — Thomas Alison Scott, pioneer of the Australian sugar-cane industry, its founder; grew after the 1905 station.

name: TELOPEA
history: The botanical name of the waratah — NSW's flower — chosen for the 1925 station among the Dundas orchards.

name: TEMPE
history: Named for the Vale of Tempe in Greece, via Alexander Brodie Spark's 1830s villa 'Tempe House' on the Cooks River, which still stands.

name: TENNYSON
history: Named for the poet laureate — Hawkesbury farm country.

name: TENNYSON POINT
history: The poet's name on the Parramatta River point, via the 1890s estate.

name: TERREY HILLS
history: Named for landholders Obadiah Terrey and Samuel Hills — their names simply joined; market gardens on the forest plateau.

name: THE DEVILS WILDERNESS
history: Named by botanist George Caley on his attempted 1804 mountain crossing — 'the dreary appearance, abruptness, intricate, and dangerous route… induced me to call it the Devil's Wilderness'; rugged Grose gorge country.

name: THE OAKS
history: Named for the she-oaks of the Burragorang road — the Cowpastures' timber village.

name: THE PONDS
history: Named for the chain of ponds on Second Ponds Creek — 1790s farm country, 2000s growth suburb.

name: THE ROCKS
history: Named for the rugged sandstone spurs the convicts first camped on in 1788 — Australia's first neighbourhood, saved from demolition by the green bans of the 1970s.

name: THE SLOPES
history: A descriptive name for the rural foothill slopes east of Kurrajong — who bestowed it is not recorded.

name: THERESA PARK
history: From the early estate 'Teresa Park' of merchant John Terry Hughes — the spelling drifted to 'Theresa' around 1860, and the identity of Theresa herself is not reliably recorded.

name: THIRLMERE
history: Named for the Lake District's Thirlmere when the railway loop opened — its lakes and steam museum keep the holiday air.

name: THORNLEIGH
history: Named for constable John Thorn, rewarded (with Horne of Hornsby) for capturing bushrangers in 1830 — his 'leigh' joined the orchard line.

name: TOONGABBIE
history: From a Dharug word for 'place near the water' — the colony's third settlement (1792), its convict farm gangs Pemulwuy's first target.

name: TREGEAR
history: From the 'Tregear' homestead of the Lethbridge family, named after their Cornish property; RAAF land in 1942, Mount Druitt housing from the 1960s.

name: TURRAMURRA
history: From a Garigal word usually given as 'high hill' or 'big hill' — the north shore line's 1890s orchard heights.

name: TURRELLA
history: From an Aboriginal word for the Wolli Creek reach, with the 1931 station.

name: ULOOLA
history: From a Dharawal-country word given as 'sun' — via Uloola Brook, Falls, Swamp and Turrets in Royal National Park.

name: ULTIMO
history: Named for surgeon John Harris's 1803 'Ultimo House' — a legal joke: a court martial against him collapsed over the clerk's error writing 'ultimo' (last month) for 'instant', so he named his estate for the word that saved him.

name: UMINA BEACH
history: From an Aboriginal word of the Woy Woy peninsula given as 'to rest' or 'place of sleep'; first subdivided 1914.

name: UPPER COLO
history: The upper reaches of the Colo River — its name said to derive from a local word for koala, though this is not certain; farmed from the early 1800s.

name: VALLEY HEIGHTS
history: The literal heights above the Nepean valley — the railway's mountain bank-engine depot.

name: VARROVILLE
history: Named for Dr Robert Townson's 1810s 'Varro Ville' farm, honouring the Roman agricultural writer Varro.

name: VAUCLUSE
history: Named for Sir Henry Browne Hayes's 1803 estate 'Vaucluse', itself named for the Fontaine de Vaucluse in France. He ringed his house with Irish turf to repel snakes.

name: VILLAWOOD
history: Coined in 1922 by inverting 'Woodville Road' (to avoid clashing with Woodville near Newcastle); its postwar migrant hostel received hundreds of thousands of arrivals and is now the detention centre.

name: VINEYARD
history: Named for the district's pioneer vineyards along the Hawkesbury road — the grape name outlived the vines by two centuries.

name: VOYAGER POINT
history: Named in memory of the 82 sailors who died when HMAS Voyager collided with HMAS Melbourne in 1964 — via the commemorative Voyager Park opened in 1965.

name: WAGSTAFFE
history: Named for Captain George Wagstaffe (1853-1920), whose land at the point was subdivided in 1904 and 1906; the Mulhall family had earlier farmed nearby at 'Mount Pleasant'.

name: WAHROONGA
history: From a Dharug word given as 'our home' — the 1890s north shore line's garden crown.

name: WAITARA
history: A Maori name from New Zealand's Taranaki, transplanted by an 1880s estate agent to the Hornsby line.

name: WAKELEY
history: Named in honour of Daniel Wakeley, a free settler of the district; developed 1980 in Fairfield.

name: WALLACIA
history: Named for grazier Robert Wallace, who from the 1880s ran cattle on land he rented along the Nepean; 'Wallacia' was adopted officially in 1906 as a compromise after both 'Wallace' and 'Boondah' were rejected.

name: WAREEMBA
history: From an Aboriginal word for the Iron Cove shore, applied by 1910s subdividers.

name: WARRAGAMBA
history: From a Gundungurra word for the river — the dam-builders' town of the 1940s–60s that drowned the Burragorang Valley.

name: WARRAWEE
history: From an Aboriginal phrase given as 'stop here' — which the 1900s railway obligingly did.

name: WARRIEWOOD
history: An Aboriginal-styled estate name for the beach valley's glasshouse farms.

name: WARRIMOO
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'eagle's nest', chosen for the 1918 mountain station.

name: WARUMBUL
history: Most likely from the 'Warumbul' guest house the National Park trustees built in 1891 — the word taken from Ridley's Kamilaroi wordlists, where it is given as 'watercourse', used of the Milky Way.

name: WARWICK FARM
history: Named after J.H. Stroud's 1804 grant, which he called Warwick Farm after Warwick in England; the estate later gave its name to the racecourse, racing's western home since 1889.

name: WATERFALL
history: Named for the falls on its creek — the last station before the Royal National Park's wilderness, and the 1900s TB sanatorium's site.

name: WATERLOO
history: Named for the 1815 battle, fresh news when the swamp mills were granted; two centuries of industry, then Green Square's towers.

name: WATSONS BAY
history: Named for Robert Watson, First Fleet quartermaster turned harbour pilot. A pilots' and fishing village from the 1790s — Sydney's oldest.

name: WATTAMOLLA
history: Recorded as 'Watta-Mowlee' by Flinders when he and Bass sheltered here in 1796 (their 'Providential Cove'), commonly given as 'place near running water' — though no verified translation exists.

name: WATTLE GROVE
history: A modern descriptive name chosen when the Holsworthy Village housing area was renamed in 1992; opened by Prime Minister Bob Hawke in 1991.

name: WAVERLEY
history: Named for Walter Scott's novel Waverley via Barnett Levey's 1827 'Waverley House' — the municipality that gave Bondi its council.

name: WAVERTON
history: Named for the Waverton estate of the Carr family (a Cheshire name); its harbour edge keeps the coal loader and BP parklands.

name: WEDDERBURN
history: A Scottish border name carried to the 1810s Campbelltown grants — airfield and gorge country.

name: WENDOREE PARK
history: Named around the 1950s by Athol Spring for his planned 'dream village' of 250 cottages on Mangrove Creek — the meaning of 'Wendoree' is unrecorded; it grew instead as a weekenders' community.

name: WENTWORTH POINT
history: Named for D'Arcy Wentworth, whose Home Bush estate met the river here — apartment city since the 2000s.

name: WENTWORTHVILLE
history: Named for D'Arcy Wentworth's 'Fitzwilliam Place' estate lands — the railway took his family name in 1885.

name: WEROMBI
history: Origin not reliably documented — generally taken as Aboriginal (Dharug/Gundungurra country) but no recorded meaning survives; a timber and farming district of Wollondilly.

name: WERRINGTON
history: Named for 'Werrington', the house and estate built around 1827 by Mary King (youngest daughter of Governor Philip Gidley King) and her husband Robert Copeland Lethbridge — Western Sydney commuter country today.

name: WERRINGTON COUNTY
history: The 'county' estate release of Werrington's farms, 1980s.

name: WERRINGTON DOWNS
history: Werrington's downs release, 1980s.

name: WEST HOXTON
history: The western extension of Hoxton Park — an 1887 syndicate's name recalling Hoxton in London; gazetted 1972.

name: WEST PENNANT HILLS
history: The western, orchard-growing part of the older Pennant Hills district, whose name is generally attributed to the English botanist Thomas Pennant (a friend of Joseph Banks) rather than to any signal flag.

name: WEST PYMBLE
history: Pymble's bushland west, toward the Lane Cove valley.

name: WEST RYDE
history: Ryde's railway-workshop west, with the 1890s pumping station.

name: WESTLEIGH
history: A 1960s coinage — the 'west lea' of Thornleigh.

name: WESTMEAD
history: The 'west mead' (meadow) of Parramatta's government domain — now the southern hemisphere's largest hospital precinct.

name: WETHERILL PARK
history: Named for the Wetherill family of the 1880s estates — Smithfield's industrial neighbour.

name: WHALAN
history: Named for James Whalan, granted 300 acres at Mount Druitt in 1831 — his father led Macquarie's Light Horse Guard; a 1960s–70s Housing Commission suburb.

name: WHALE BEACH
history: Named for the whale-shaped rock (or beached whales — accounts differ) of the peninsula's second-last beach.

name: WHEELER HEIGHTS
history: Named for James Wheeler, whose 1840s family farmed the Narrabeen creek that keeps their name.

name: WHEENY CREEK
history: From a Darug name recorded on Rev. John McGarvie's 1829 list of 178 Aboriginal place names along Dyarubbin (the Hawkesbury) — one of the few to survive on modern maps, though no meaning was recorded.

name: WILBERFORCE
history: One of Macquarie's five towns (1810), named for abolitionist William Wilberforce.

name: WILEY PARK
history: Named for the 20-acre reserve bequeathed in 1906 by shoemaker J.V. Wiley for a public recreation ground — the railway station, opened 1938, took the park's name.

name: WILLMOT
history: Named for Thomas Willmot, first president of Blacktown Shire Council (1906–10); established 1971 in the Mount Druitt scheme.

name: WILLOUGHBY
history: Named in the 1830s, most likely by surveyor-general Mitchell for Sir James Willoughby Gordon; the brick-and-quarry municipality of the middle north shore.

name: WILLOUGHBY EAST
history: Willoughby's eastern quarter by the Flat Rock gully brickpits.

name: WILTON
history: Named around 1840 by magistrate William Antill after his nearby 'Wilton' estate (itself echoing Wilton in England); the village's town lots were first sold in 1844.

name: WINDSOR
history: Macquarie's chief Hawkesbury town (1810), named for the Thames-side royal town — its Georgian square and St Matthew's are Greenway's country masterpieces.

name: WINDSOR DOWNS
history: Windsor's sandy downs, rural-residential since the 1980s.

name: WINMALEE
history: An Aboriginal-styled name chosen by ballot in 1972 for the growing mountains suburb.

name: WINSTON HILLS
history: Named in the 1960s for Winston Churchill — the Hills District's tribute estate.

name: WOLLI CREEK
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'camp' or 'place of pines' — the creek valley kept wild by freeway-fighters, the suburb born 2000s at its mouth.

name: WOLLSTONECRAFT
history: Named for Edward Wollstonecraft — nephew of feminist Mary Wollstonecraft and first cousin of Frankenstein author Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley — whose 1821 grant of 500 acres covered these slopes.

name: WONDABYNE
history: From Mount Wondabyne in Darkinjung country — presumably Aboriginal, though no reliable meaning survives; its 1889 quarry station remains Australia's only railway station with no road access.

name: WOODBINE
history: Named after Woodbine Cottage, home of the prominent Payten family from the 1870s, on land at Campbelltown's edge.

name: WOODCROFT
history: A 1990s estate coinage on the Eastern Creek farms.

name: WOODPARK
history: The wooded park corner of the Prospect farms.

name: WOOLLAHRA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'camp' or 'meeting ground' — chosen in 1856 for Daniel Cooper's grand estate; the municipality of the eastern villages.

name: WOOLLOOMOOLOO
history: From a Gadigal word, possibly 'walla-mulla' — place of plenty, or a young black kangaroo. Subdivided from the 1840s into the harbourside working quarter it remains.

name: WOOLOOWARE
history: From a Dharawal word given as 'muddy flat' — the Cronulla line's oyster-shore station.

name: WOOLWICH
history: Named after London's dockyard district — fittingly: Mort's Dock and the great Woolwich Dock cut its sandstone peninsula.

name: WORONORA
history: From a Dharawal word for the river — 'black rock' by some accounts; the river village reached by its 1940s suspension footbridge.

name: WORONORA DAM
history: Named for the dam built across the Woronora River 1927–41 — the river name first recorded as 'Wooloonora' in 1827, often glossed 'black rock', though the records don't support a firm meaning.

name: WORONORA HEIGHTS
history: The plateau above the black-rock river.

name: WOY WOY
history: From a Darkinjung word given as 'deep water' or 'big lagoon' — the tidal channel beside the town, which grew with the railway in the late 1880s.

name: WOY WOY BAY
history: The adjoining bay of the 'deep water' — originally Webb's Flat, after the Brisbane Water district's first European settler.

name: YAGOONA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'now' or 'today', chosen in 1927 for the Bankstown line station.

name: YARRAMUNDI
history: Named for Yarramundi, the Boorooberongal elder who met Phillip in 1791 — his daughter Maria Lock became a celebrated figure of early Aboriginal history.

name: YARRAWARRAH
history: From a Dharawal word of the Woronora plateau scrub.

name: YATTALUNGA
history: Assumed to derive from 'yattala', given as 'cascade' — but that word is from South Australia's Kaurna language, so the name was likely imported rather than local Darkinjung; settled after the early-1900s subdivisions.

name: YELLOW ROCK
history: A descriptive name from the yellow Hawkesbury-sandstone outcrops that built many local heritage buildings; assigned 1972.

name: YENABILLI
history: One of the Royal National Park localities gazetted in 2022, named after Yenabilli Point near Maianbar — presumably Dharawal, but no reliable recorded meaning could be found.

name: YENNORA
history: From an Aboriginal word given as 'to stroll', with the 1927 station and the wool stores that followed.

name: YOWIE BAY
history: From a Dharawal word ('echo', by most accounts — the yowie monster connection is folk etymology) for the Port Hacking bay of 1880s pleasure grounds.

name: ZETLAND
history: Named in the 1870s after the Earl of Zetland (an old form of Shetland) by his relative, Governor Sir Hercules Robinson, who gave the name to his Waterloo estate; later home to the Victoria Park racecourse and factories, and now Green Square.
